definition: A lookup table (LUT) that remaps each RGB channel independently along a single tone curve — ideal for transfer-function changes (e.g. log to linear, or applying a gamma) but unable to alter hue or saturation because the channels never interact. Often paired with a matrix and a 3D LUT for a full transform.

Reports jointly to the Editor and Post Supervisor. Manages the Avid/edit project, ingests dailies, syncs sound, and pulls EDL/XML/AAF for turnover to VFX and finishing. First point of contact for offline editorial queries.

A single-frame 1 kHz sync beep placed exactly 2 seconds before first frame of action (on the '2' of a countdown) to align picture and sound. A matching tail pop sits after the last frame. The universal sync check for audio deliverables.

The telecine cadence that maps 24 fps film to 29.97 fps interlaced video: the film is slowed 0.1% to 23.976 fps, then each set of four film frames is spread across ten video fields in a repeating 3-2 (three fields, then two) field pattern. It produces the interlaced 60i look from progressive film.

A lookup table (LUT) addressed by all three RGB values at once, mapping points on a 3D lattice (e.g. 17^3, 33^3, 65^3) with interpolation between them. Because the channels interact it can change hue and saturation, making it the standard way to bake a creative look or display transform; precision depends on lattice size.

Channel-based surround layouts. 5.1 = L, C, R, Ls, Rs plus the .1 LFE; 7.1 adds side/back surround pairs (L, C, R, Lss, Rss, Lrs, Rrs, LFE). The '.1' is the band-limited LFE (Low-Frequency Effects) channel feeding the subwoofer.

Immersive speaker layouts written as floor.LFE.height: 7.1.4 = 7 surround + 1 LFE + 4 ceiling; 9.1.6 = 9 + 1 + 6, used on larger theatrical/mastering rigs. An Atmos renderer folds the same master down to whatever monitoring layout is present (9.1.6, 7.1.4, 5.1.4, etc.).

A rich binary interchange format from the AMWA (Advanced Media Workflow Association, now standardised via SMPTE) that carries multi-track timelines, effects, keyframes, gain/EQ, and optionally embedded or linked media. AAF is the standard way to move an Avid/Pro Tools project between picture and sound, or into a finishing/grading system, where an EDL would be too limited.

PQ's defining property: a code value maps to a fixed brightness in nits regardless of display (a PQ value of 100 always means 100 nits). This is a fundamental shift from SDR, where brightness was always relative to the display.

The Academy of Motion Picture Arts and Sciences' open, device-independent colour-management framework that brings every camera, VFX render and display into one defined, scene-linear pipeline. Footage is converted in via an Input Transform, worked in ACES spaces, then rendered to each display via an Output Transform, with ACES2065-1 as the archival master encoding.

A sidecar XML file (spec S-2019-001) that records everything needed to rebuild an ACES viewing pipeline — the Input Transform, any looks (CDL values or referenced CLF/look files) and the Output Transform. It replaces the older ACESclip format and travels with shots to guarantee consistent colour intent.

The full creative-plus-display pipeline applied to ACES data for viewing: any Look Transform (LMT) first, then the rendering and device output (RRT + ODT, or the single ACES 2.0 Output Transform). It defines exactly how scene-linear ACES is rendered to a given display.

The neutral reference shared by all ACES colour spaces, at CIE 1931 chromaticity x = 0.32168, y = 0.33767. Often loosely called 'D60', but there is no CIE D60 standard illuminant: it is a ~6000K daylight-derived point deliberately set slightly off the daylight locus, so do not confuse it with display white points such as D65.

The core ACES interchange and archival colour space, defined in SMPTE ST 2065-1. It is scene-linear (gamma 1.0), uses the AP0 primaries and the ACES white point (approximately D60), and is stored as 16-bit half-float; it is the master format you archive and exchange in, not the space you grade or render in.

A logarithmic ACES grading space on the AP1 primaries, designed so colour-grading tools (lift/gamma/gain, curves) feel like traditional log workflows. It is a pure log encoding with no toe, so it has a hard floor in the deep shadows compared with ACEScct.

A log ACES grading space on AP1, identical to ACEScc above the break point (ACES value 0.0078125) but with an added 'toe' in the shadows so lift operations 'milk' or fog like a film scan. Added in ACES 1.0.3 on colourist feedback; generally the default grading space unless there's a reason to use ACEScc.

The scene-linear working colour space for VFX rendering and compositing, using the ACES AP1 primaries (a wide but realisable gamut with no negative values). Renders and comps are done in ACEScg so light maths and gamut behave correctly; it is a subset of the wider ACES2065-1 archival space.

Audio Definition Model Broadcast Wave Format — the single-file Dolby Atmos master. A standard WAV containing all bed and object audio plus ADM metadata (positions, routing) that lets any Atmos renderer recreate the mix exactly. The Atmos deliverable that replaces a channel-based printmaster.

Automated Dialogue Replacement: re-recording an actor's lines in a studio after the shoot, performed in sync to picture, to fix unusable production audio, change a performance, or make censorship/airline edits. Also called looping or post-sync; 'automated' refers to the rig that loops the picture cue, not to any AI process.

Active Format Description — metadata (per SMPTE ST 2016-1 / ETSI TS 101 154) signalling the intended aspect ratio and protected area within a video frame, telling downstream displays how to crop or pillarbox. Prevents wrong zooming of 16:9 content on 4:3 or vice versa.

Physically isolating a system or network from the internet and untrusted networks so assets cannot be exfiltrated remotely. Common in high-security finishing, VFX vaults and DI (Digital Intermediate) suites, and a recognised TPN (Trusted Partner Network) control for the most sensitive content.

BAFTA's screen-sustainability framework and carbon calculator, accepted by Netflix, Amazon, BBC, Channel 4 and many NZ commissioners. It measures energy, travel, materials, accommodation and post (cloud compute, suites), with certification running from footprint-only up to 3 stars (third-party verified).

A tab-delimited text file listing clip metadata — clip name, tape/reel, source timecode, frame rate, and custom columns — used to import or reconcile shot data in Avid and other systems. Commonly generated by the DIT or dailies house so editorial inherits accurate naming and metadata.

Amazon's per-title delivery defined in the Amazon Studios Asset portal (portal.amazonstudios.com). IMF is the preferred mezzanine; HDR10, HDR10+ and Dolby Vision are all accepted; audio for originals targets -24 LKFS dialog-gated with true peak ≤ -2 dBTP; captions in IMSC 1.1/TTML.

A lens that optically squeezes a wide field horizontally onto the sensor, then is 'desqueezed' in monitoring and post to a wide aspect (classic 2x desqueezes to 2.39:1 scope). Squeeze factors include 2x (classic), 1.8x (e.g. Cooke FF SF), 1.5x and 1.33x; anamorphics give oval bokeh, horizontal flares and fuller use of vertical sensor resolution.

Arbitrary Output Variable — an individual render pass output by the renderer alongside the main 'beauty', e.g. diffuse, specular, reflection, depth (Z), normals, position, or object/material IDs. Lets compositors adjust lighting and looks without re-rendering the CG.

The two sets of RGB primaries used in ACES. AP0 is the interchange gamut of ACES2065-1, whose triangle fully encloses the entire CIE 1931 visible spectral locus so every visible colour encodes with positive values. AP1 is a tighter, more practical working gamut (close to Rec.2020) used by ACEScg, ACEScc and ACEScct.

The Australasian music-rights bodies that license public performance/communication (APRA) and mechanical reproduction (AMCOS) of musical works across Australia and New Zealand. Productions clear and report music cue usage to them for composed and library music rights.

ARRI large-format cinema camera with a 4448 x 3096 (LF Open Gate) sensor recording 12-bit log ARRIRAW (.ari) or ProRes in Log-C3 / ARRI Wide Gamut to Codex SXR Capture Drives. A common HETV/feature A-camera.

ARRI's camera colour space paired with Log C: AWG3 (ARRI Wide Gamut 3) for LogC3 cameras, AWG4 (ARRI Wide Gamut 4) for the ALEXA 35/LogC4. A wide working gamut that comfortably contains Rec.709, P3 and Rec.2020, used for grading and as the ACES IDT input.

ARRI's uncompressed (or losslessly compressed via HDE) Bayer RAW format, the highest-fidelity ARRI capture and the VFX-preferred negative. On the ALEXA 35 (ALEV 4 sensor) the sensor data is 18-bit wide and quantised to 13 bits, carrying up to 17 stops of dynamic range in ARRI Wide Gamut 4; earlier ALEXA models (e.g. LF) record 12-bit log ARRIRAW.

A Tektronix scope display plotting luma against total chroma amplitude in an arrowhead shape, used to confirm combined luma-plus-chroma stays within composite-legal limits. It complements the Diamond display for full gamut compliance.

A frame-/timecode-accurate transcript of the finished program exactly as it aired — every line of dialogue, on-screen text, song and credit, with timecodes. It is a standard delivery item used for localisation, captioning, dubbing, music-cue confirmation and archive/legal records.
seeAlso: Subtitles vs SDH vs Closed Captions

definition: An open ASC specification (v1.0 released April 2023) that records media-file and folder checksums in a human-readable XML, building a verifiable chain of custody from on-set offload to archive. It supports xxHash, MD5, SHA1, SHA256 and C4 hashes; each copy adds a new generation so any tampering or bit-rot is detectable.

aka: American Society of Cinematographers Color Decision List
definition: An ASC (American Society of Cinematographers) interchange format carrying ten numbers — Slope, Offset and Power per RGB channel plus a single Saturation value — to communicate primary grade decisions between on-set, dailies and the grading suite. It is device-agnostic and travels in EDLs, ALE and XML so a colourist can reproduce the set look exactly.

The width-to-height ratio of the frame. Common finishing ratios: 16:9 (1.78:1) for broadcast/streaming, 1.85:1 theatrical flat, 2.39:1 (often called 2.40) DCI scope, and 2.00:1 increasingly used for streaming. Drives framing, lens choice (spherical vs anamorphic) and delivery masking.

High-speed file-transfer platforms that use UDP-based acceleration (Aspera's FASP, Signiant's equivalent) to move large media — such as overseas 4K negative scans back to the NZ dailies house — far faster than standard FTP. Bandwidth and account access must be tested before a 35mm shoot day.

IMF/DCP structural XML: ASSETMAP indexes every file in the package by UUID and physical location; VOLINDEX gives the volume number for multi-volume packages. ASSETMAP must sit at the drive/package root.

An international customs document allowing temporary, duty-free export and re-import of cameras, lenses and exposed negative leaving and returning to NZ. Issued by the NZ Chamber of Commerce (allow 5-10 working days, valid up to 12 months); it must be presented and stamped at every border the goods cross.

The US loudness practice underpinning the CALM Act; it recommends an anchor (dialogue) loudness target of -24 LKFS for content using a fixed dialnorm. The North American counterpart to EBU R128, differing mainly in the -24 vs -23 target and gating details.
A narrated description track for blind and low-vision viewers, delivered as a separate 48 kHz/24-bit WAV mix. An access deliverable now treated as first-class alongside subtitles and captions.

Digimetrics/Telestream automated QC engine used in file-based broadcast workflows, typically integrated into a media-processing system to verify files against delivery rules before transcode or delivery.

Autodesk's secure cloud dailies/review platform, formed by merging PIX and Moxion. HDR-aware (Dolby Vision/HDR10), supports synchronised live 'Rooms', DRM (digital rights management) and forensic watermarking — favoured for studio productions needing maximum security.

File-based quality control where software scans a finished media file against a rule template, flagging technical faults (illegal levels, gamut errors, freezes, dropouts, loudness, cadence, blockiness) without a human watching every frame. It catches measurable, machine-detectable problems fast but cannot judge creative or editorial intent.
Royalty-free next-generation long-GOP streaming codec (MP4/WebM) adopted by Netflix and YouTube. A distribution format, not a mastering format.
Intra-frame (each frame compressed independently) H.264/AVC-based acquisition codec used by cameras such as the Panasonic Varicam 35, recorded in V-Gamut / V-Log.

Avid's intra-frame mezzanine codecs (the VC-3 family), the practical equivalent of ProRes in Avid/PC pipelines. DNxHD is the HD-only generation; DNxHR is the resolution-independent successor with quality tiers (LB, SQ, HQ, HQX 12-bit, 444). VC-3 is defined by SMPTE ST 2019-1 and standardised for use in IMF by SMPTE ST 2067-70 (2024).
Sony's recorder/media system for Venice: the AXS-R7 recorder writing to AXS A-Series memory cards (512 GB / 1 TB), read via the AXS Card Reader.
Visible stepped bands in what should be a smooth gradient, caused by insufficient bit depth or heavy compression. A common dailies and automated-QC flag.

Interra Systems' automated file-based QC platform, common in broadcast and streaming delivery. It ships with delivery templates (Netflix, DPP, iTunes, etc.) and uses machine learning for checks like loudness, lip-sync and language detection alongside standard technical checks.

Most cinema sensors are single-chip Bayer arrays carrying a colour filter mosaic of 50% green, 25% red, 25% blue photosites. Demosaicing (debayering) is the algorithm that reconstructs a full RGB value per pixel from this mosaic, which is why a 6K Bayer sensor does not resolve a true 6K of luminance detail.

The main, fully-shaded combined render of a CG element — all lighting components summed into the 'final' look — as opposed to the separate AOVs broken out beside it. The compositor's starting layer for an element.

The two element types in Dolby Atmos. Beds are channel-based stems locked to fixed speaker positions (the home Atmos bed maxes out at 7.1.2); objects carry their own positional metadata (x, y, z plus size) and are rendered dynamically to wherever speakers physically sit — up to 118 objects alongside a single bed.

The number of bits per colour channel, setting how many tonal steps are recorded: 8-bit = 256, 10-bit = 1,024, 12-bit = 4,096, 16-bit = 65,536 per channel. Higher bit depth resists banding and survives aggressive grading; log and RAW generally need 10-bit minimum, 12-16-bit for high-end work.

Blackmagic's partially-processed RAW codec with two encoding modes: constant bitrate (fixed ratios 3:1, 5:1, 8:1, 12:1) for predictable file sizes, or constant quality (Q0/Q1/Q3/Q5) which varies bitrate to hold image quality. White balance, ISO and Gen 5 colour science remain editable metadata.

The black bars added when an image of one aspect ratio sits inside a different raster (e.g. 2.39 inside 16:9). Blanking must be flat black at the spec'd code values (legal 16,16,16 or full 0,0,0); Netflix/Amazon reject blanking with infra-red/noise contamination or wrong levels.

TPN's two assessment tiers. Blue Shield is a self-reported questionnaire submitted to the registry; Gold Shield additionally requires an independent assessment by a TPN-accredited assessor and is the higher trust level studios increasingly demand. Each is scoped to a site (facility), application, or cloud.

A directional microphone (typically shotgun) on a handheld pole positioned just out of frame to capture dialogue from above or below. The preferred primary dialogue source for its natural sound and perspective.

Signal kept within the range broadcasters accept — luma and RGB within published tolerances and audio within loudness/peak limits — so it does not clip, bloom or buzz on transmission. In practice, a pass against the relevant levels and gamut spec (e.g. EBU R103).

ITU-R naming convention: 'BT' is the series designation for television-broadcasting standards, and 'Rec.' (or 'REC') is short for 'Recommendation', the ITU's term for a published standard. Hence BT.709 and Rec.709 are the same document.

ITU-R BT.1886, the standardised gamma EOTF (~2.4) for SDR flat-panel displays, defined to mimic the response of the old CRT. It is the SDR reference curve, typically at a ~100 cd/m² reference white in a grading suite.

On-screen text overlaid on a viewing copy — timecode, frame number, reel/clip name, version or watermark — to aid communication and tracking during turnovers and reviews. Burn-ins live only on reference copies, never on the conformed master.

US law (the Commercial Advertisement Loudness Mitigation Act, in force since December 2012) requiring TV commercials to be no louder than the programme they accompany, enforced by mandating ATSC A/85. It is the legal driver behind the -24 LKFS US broadcast delivery target.

The recording media used inside the camera (cards or capture drives). Treated as precious, irreplaceable assets and only wiped after both QC and the LTO (Linear Tape-Open) archive are verified by the lab; roughly 30 days of mags per camera is the recommended buffer.

The native wide colour gamut a camera encodes alongside its log curve (e.g. Sony S-Gamut3/S-Gamut3.Cine, ARRI Wide Gamut 3/4, REDWideGamutRGB). The log curve plus camera gamut together fully describe the recording, and both are mapped to ACES by the Input Transform.

A logarithmic transfer curve cameras use to pack high dynamic range into a limited bit depth for recording (e.g. ARRI LogC3/LogC4, Sony S-Log3, RED Log3G10, Panasonic V-Log). It is paired with a wide camera gamut, and together they are the data an ACES Input Transform converts into scene-linear ACES.

The unaltered footage as recorded by the camera (RAW, ProRes or MXF straight off the card) — the highest-quality source the online conforms back to. Called OCN (Original Camera Negative) in film-era language or OCF (Original Camera Files) for digital.

Projecting a 2D image (a photo or matte painting) onto simple 3D geometry from the camera's viewpoint, so a still gains parallax and supports a limited camera move. The 2.5D bridge between flat matte painting and full CG environments.

Unprocessed or lightly compressed sensor data recorded before debayering, white balance and ISO are baked in, giving the post pipeline full latitude to set those parameters later. It trades large file sizes and heavier processing for maximum flexibility versus a baked codec like ProRes.

The per-roll or per-card log generated on set recording every clip with its scene/take, timecode, lens, filtration, frame rate, ISO/EI, white balance and any circle-takes or notes. It is the primary metadata reference for editorial, the grade and VFX.

Workflow where a proxy of the camera feed is encoded on set (e.g. by a Teradek/Serv device) and uploaded in near-real-time to a cloud review platform, putting graded dailies in editors' and producers' hands the same day.
Canon's log transfer curves (Log 2 for maximum latitude, Log 3 for an easier grade) used on Cinema EOS bodies such as the C500 Mk II and C700, paired with the Cinema Gamut camera colour space.

The North American closed-caption standards. CEA-608 ("line 21") is the legacy analogue/SD caption system; CEA-708 is its digital-TV successor, supporting multiple caption services, fonts, colours and positioning. Modern files carry this data inside SCC, MXF ANC (ancillary-data) tracks, or as payloads embedded in IMSC/SMPTE-TT.
seeAlso: Subtitles vs SDH vs Closed Captions

definition: Tektronix's file-based QC system (now under Telestream), long a broadcast-facility standard; no longer sold new but still in service in many plants and often cited as the legacy benchmark for automated checks.
High-speed flash card standard (Type B) used by cameras such as the Canon C500 Mk II and Blackmagic URSA Mini Pro, in capacities up to roughly 4 TB, read over USB-C/CFexpress readers.

Computer-generated imagery output by a renderer (Arnold, RenderMan, V-Ray, etc.) from 3D scenes — characters, props, environments, FX. Delivered to comp as multichannel EXRs carrying the beauty plus AOVs for flexible integration.

The documented, unbroken record of every transfer and copy of the media from camera to archive, proving nothing was lost or altered. In modern workflows it is carried by ASC MHL files plus offload and lab reports.

The complete, documented trail of legal ownership in a production: option/purchase of underlying material, writer and life-rights agreements, music and footage licences, and assignments, proving the producer actually owns or has cleared everything in the finished work. Gaps make a film unsaleable and uninsurable, so distributors and E&O underwriters demand it clean.

A document generated by editorial that itemises exactly what changed between two versions of a turnover — frames added or removed, shots re-timed, scenes re-ordered — so VFX, sound and DI update only the affected work instead of re-conforming from scratch. Essential once picture lock starts drifting.

A fingerprint computed from a file's contents (MD5, SHA, or fast xxHash) used to verify that a delivery copied or transferred without corruption — matching hashes mean identical files. A DCP's PKL embeds hashes for exactly this reason, and on-set/DIT offloads (Silverstack, Hedge) verify against them.

Compression that keeps full luma (brightness) resolution but reduces colour (chroma) resolution, exploiting the eye's lower sensitivity to colour detail. Written J:a:b — 4:4:4 is full colour, 4:2:2 halves horizontal chroma, 4:2:0 halves it horizontally and vertically. Mastering and VFX want 4:4:4 or 4:2:2; consumer delivery is usually 4:2:0.

The device-independent reference colour model defined by the Commission Internationale de l'Eclairage (CIE) in 1931, based on human colour-matching experiments. Its 2D chromaticity diagram (the "horseshoe") is the universal map on which all gamuts are plotted and compared.

The standard overseas-lab alternative for UK-led NZ productions: Cinelab London (Slough) handles ECN-2, black-and-white, 16mm, ARRISCAN/Director 10K scanning and preservation; Cinelab Romania (Bucharest) is a cost-competitive European option.
Canon's wide camera colour gamut, paired with Canon Log on Cinema EOS cameras.
Canon's lighter-weight raw codec for the C500 Mk II / C700, recorded in Cinema Gamut with Canon Log 2 or Log 3. Used on some dramas and documentaries.

A frame or shot of the scene captured with the rig, actor, marker or unwanted object removed, used to paint or patch over those elements in the comp. Often shot as a lock-off (static camera) immediately before or after the main take.

The process of obtaining legal permission to use every third-party element on screen and in the mix: music, archival footage, brands/logos, artwork, locations, names and likenesses. Documented clearances feed the chain of title and are a precondition of E&O cover and delivery.

An Academy XML-based file format (spec S-2014-006, now being standardised by SMPTE) that chains arbitrary colour operators — matrices, 1D/3D LUTs, ASC-CDL, log/exponential shapers — into a single portable transform. It lets a complete look or transform move accurately between tools.
US broadcast captions in the CEA-608 (legacy analogue) and CEA-708 (digital) standards, carried in the master or as a sidecar SCC file. The US-domestic equivalent of SDH.

A Group Of Pictures in long-GOP video (e.g. MPEG-2, H.264) that does not reference frames outside its own group, so it can be cut or spliced cleanly at GOP boundaries. Often mandated in broadcast delivery specs versus open GOP.

A security baseline for remote review: per-user MFA plus SSO, forensic and visible watermark on every proxy, downloads disabled unless the distributor permits, access logs kept 90+ days, auto-expiring review windows, and device attestation where available.

A post pipeline built on cloud storage/compute and remote workstations rather than local kit. A typical 2026 footprint: AWS S3/EC2 or Azure for storage; Iconik or SonyCi for MAM; Avid Edit On Demand or Blackmagic Cloud for editorial; AWS Deadline Cloud for render; Parsec or NICE DCV for remote desktops.

Content Mapping Unit. The Dolby Vision content-mapping process that derives lower-target trims (100-nit SDR, 600, 1,000, 4,000 nit) from the HDR master. Two algorithm generations exist — CM v2.9 and v4.0 — and Netflix accepts both.

The de-facto standard EDL text format, originating on CMX linear edit controllers. It uses 8-character alphanumeric reel IDs, four timecodes per event (source in/out, record in/out), is effectively limited to ~999 events and one video track, and carries no effect parameters beyond basic transitions — which is why it is paired with handles and notes rather than used for full finishing.

COder-DECoder: the scheme that compresses and decompresses image data. Intra-frame (I-frame) codecs like ProRes and DNxHR compress each frame independently and scrub/edit smoothly; long-GOP codecs (H.264/H.265) compress across groups of frames for smaller files but heavier decode. The choice affects edit performance, quality and storage.
Codex camera media for ARRI ALEXA cameras: SXR Capture Drives (ALEXA LF/Mini LF, 1-2 TB) and Compact Drives (ALEXA 35, 1-4 TB), read via the Codex Compact Drive Reader.

A node or operation (notably in DaVinci Resolve) that explicitly converts between a defined input gamut/gamma and output gamut/gamma — e.g. S-Log3/S-Gamut3.Cine to ACEScct, or ACEScg to Rec.709. It is the manual alternative to a fully managed ACES project when you need precise, per-clip control.

The warmth or coolness of a light/white point expressed in kelvin (K) — e.g. ~3200K tungsten (warm), ~5600K daylight (cool) — used to set white balance so neutrals read neutral. Lower K is warmer/oranger, higher K is cooler/bluer.

The artist who sets the final look of every shot in the grade, balancing exposure and colour across the cut, building the creative grade with the DOP and director, and rendering the graded SDR and HDR masters. Typically works in DaVinci Resolve, Baselight or similar.

Combining multiple image layers — plates, CG renders, mattes, paint — into a single seamless frame. The final assembly stage of a VFX shot, built node-by-node in tools like Nuke; the artist is a compositor or 'comper'.

Any visible distortion introduced by lossy encoding (blocking, banding, ringing/mosquito noise, or smearing) produced when transform coefficients are coarsely quantised at low bitrate. QC tools detect and threshold these against the deliverable spec.

Rebuilding the locked offline timeline against the original high-resolution media using an EDL, AAF or XML, so every cut, transition and effect lands frame-accurately on the real footage. Unlike a simple relink, a conform reconstructs the timeline itself ready for grade, VFX and finishing.

Media-management operations: consolidate copies only the media actually used in a sequence (often plus handles) into one location to shrink and tidy a project, while transcode converts that media to another codec/resolution. Frequently run together when prepping a project for archive, handoff or a new edit system.
The delivery-side contact responsible for shepherding final deliverables to the broadcaster or platform. The person to consult on delivery questions.

The umbrella discipline of protecting pre-release assets (scripts, dailies, VFX plates, cuts, mixes, DCPs) from leak or theft across the supply chain. It spans access control, encryption, watermarking, secure transfer, monitored facilities, NDAs and the controls audited by TPN.

A per-shot document accompanying a turnover that lists exact frame ranges, handles, plate roles (fg/bg) and edit-vs-source timecodes for the material. Lets the vendor confirm they received the right frames and lengths.

The XML timeline of an IMF or DCP: it specifies how the video, audio and timed-text track files are sequenced and trimmed (by UUID) to build one playable version of the programme. Adding or swapping a CPL is how you create an alternate cut or language version without re-wrapping the essence.

The ratio between a reference format (usually full frame) and a smaller sensor or readout mode, expressing how much the field of view tightens. A Super 35 readout on a full-frame body crops roughly 1.4-1.5x, turning a 35mm lens into a tighter equivalent and changing which lenses cover the active area.

The rugged, removable exFAT-formatted hard-drive caddy that is the physical carrier for DCP delivery to NZ exhibitors, with the ASSETMAP at the root and no stray files. KDMs travel separately by email or portal.

An open standard that stores per-object and per-material ID mattes inside a multi-channel EXR using hashed coverage, letting compositors pull clean isolation masks for any object after rendering. Avoids pre-baking a separate matte pass for every element.

MovieLabs' reference security architecture for building and running cloud production workflows. It is a zero-trust, deny-by-default model: every user, device and service must be authenticated and every action explicitly authorised by policy before it runs.

The Academy's reference programming language for unambiguously expressing colour transforms (Input Transforms, the RRT, ODTs). It is the authoritative source definition; production tools typically implement the same maths natively or via baked LUTs/CLF rather than running CTL directly.

The per-programme list of every music cue with title, composer/publisher shares, PRO, usage type, duration and timecode in/out. Filed with APRA AMCOS or the broadcaster so composer royalties pay; late or wrong cue sheets block royalties and are a supervisor reputational issue.

The agent who lodges NZ export and receiving-country import declarations for unprocessed negative (commercial goods in transit). Neg cans are labelled 'Exposed cinematographic film — do not X-ray' so handlers hand-search or use film-safe screening.

The ~6300K projector white point of DCI-P3 cinema, at CIE coordinates (0.314, 0.351). Often loosely called "D63", but it is not a CIE daylight illuminant and sits slightly green of the Planckian locus, a legacy of optimising xenon lamp output.

The CIE standard daylight illuminant at a correlated colour temperature of approximately 6500K, used as the white point for Rec.709, Rec.2020, Rec.2100 and P3-D65. It is the default neutral white for almost all television and HDR mastering.

Reference white points. D65 (~6500 K) is the white of Rec.709, Rec.2020, P3-D65 and most video grading; D63 (~6300 K) is the warmer theatrical white used by DCI-P3 in the cinema. This difference is why a P3-D65 HDR grade and a DCI-P3 cinema grade do not match.

The processed, synced and colour-corrected (CDL/LUT-applied) footage from each shoot day, transcoded to editorial-friendly proxies and pushed to the cutting room and producers for review. Generated by the dailies lab or on-set DIT and logged in the lab/transfer report.

The on-set role focused on safely offloading camera and sound cards to redundant storage, verifying every copy with checksums and generating the chain-of-custody report. Distinct from a DIT — a wrangler does not build LUTs, configure cameras, or make colour decisions.

Digital Audio Workstation — the software environment (e.g. Avid Pro Tools, the de facto post standard) where dialogue, ADR, Foley, effects and music are edited and mixed. The audio equivalent of the picture NLE.
Decibels relative to digital full scale, where 0 dBFS is the loudest a digital signal can represent. Cinema reference monitoring places dialogue around -31 dBFS RMS.

The uncompressed, unencrypted master that precedes a DCP: TIFF frames in X'Y'Z' colour at gamma 2.6 (per SMPTE ST 428-1), plus separate uncompressed audio stems and subtitle/caption data. The format-neutral source from which the compressed, wrapped DCP is generated.

Digital Cinema Initiatives, the studio joint venture whose specification defines compliant digital cinema: 2K/4K JPEG 2000 imaging (max 250 Mbit/s), X'Y'Z' colour, AES-128 encryption, KDM key delivery and forensic marking. 'DCI-compliant' projectors and servers meet this spec.

Digital Cinema Initiatives raster family: 2K Full Container 2048x1080 and 4K 4096x2160, with Flat 1.85 (1998x1080 / 3996x2160) and Scope 2.39 (2048x858 / 4096x1716) crops. Used for theatrical DCPs, not streaming.

Digital Cinema Initiatives P3. The wide colour gamut defined for theatrical projection, larger than Rec.709 and the practical mastering volume for most HDR work. It comes in cinema (P3-DCI, ~D63 theatrical white) and P3-D65 (video) variants, and is commonly mastered inside a Rec.2020 container for HDR delivery.

A folder of files (not a single container) built to DCI/SMPTE spec for cinema ingest: JPEG 2000 picture in MXF, 24-bit PCM audio in MXF, and XML (ASSETMAP, VOLINDEX, PKL, CPL) binding it with checksums and metadata. Picture is graded in P3 at the DCI white point and delivered as 12-bit X'Y'Z' at gamma 2.6.

Software/hardware that builds Digital Cinema Packages: Rohde & Schwarz Clipster (tier-1, used by Park Road, Images & Sound, Deluxe); EasyDCP Creator (Fraunhofer, mid-tier boutique); DaVinci Resolve Studio (built-in JPEG2000 encode, indie); MTI Cortex (doc/festival); DCP-o-matic (free, open-source). Always QC the finished DCP on a real DCI-compliant projector before release.

Compositing using deep images (introduced in OpenEXR 2.0) that store a list of colour/opacity samples at multiple depths per pixel rather than a single flat value. Lets overlapping elements, volumes and holdouts combine accurately without hard-edged mattes, and avoids re-rendering when objects intersect.

The supervisor's grid tracking each deliverable (picture HDR/SDR IMF, Dolby Vision XML, 5.1 and Atmos masters, M&E, stems, textless, subtitles, captions, proxy, QC report, LTO archive report) across due, status, QC and delivered states.

The contractual document from a broadcaster, distributor or streamer defining every master, format, codec, loudness target, caption, metadata field and document required for acceptance (in the UK/EU often based on the DPP, the Digital Production Partnership, standard). It is the authoritative checklist post must build and QC against; missing items delay acceptance and payment.

Dialogue Normalization — metadata in Dolby (AC-3/Dolby Digital, AC-4) bitstreams telling the decoder the programme's average dialogue loudness so playback gain auto-adjusts to a consistent level across content. An integer 1–31 maps to -1 to -31 LKFS, set from a dialogue-gated measurement of the mix.

Cleaning and organising production dialogue — removing noises, smoothing edits, filling room tone, splitting tracks per character/mic — before the mix. The dialogue editor decides which lines are usable and which must go to ADR.

Loudness measured only during dialogue, so the figure reflects speech level rather than mix-wide energy. Netflix delivery requires -27 LKFS dialogue-gated (±2 LU), falling back to -24 LKFS integrated when there is too little dialogue to gate reliably.

A Tektronix scope display that combines R, G and B into a diamond shape so out-of-gamut RGB errors show instantly as excursions past the graticule. It is the standard tool for spotting gamut violations a waveform or vectorscope hides.

A photoreal CG replica of a real actor, stunt performer or hero object, used for shots impossible or unsafe to capture practically (big falls, crowd tiling, set extensions). Often built from on-set scanning and reference photography.

The end-to-end finishing stage where the conformed picture is colour graded and rendered to all deliverable masters. Modern DI produces multiple display versions (HDR, SDR, theatrical P3) from one graded source via trim passes and output transforms.

The named data-lab contact who processes overnight dailies: re-applies CDLs (Color Decision Lists) in an ACES pipeline, archives to LTO tape, runs lab-side QC, and outputs editorial transcodes. Listed on the Workflow Document cover sheet.

Image data already encoded for a specific display and standard (e.g. Rec.709 at 100 nits, or Rec.2100 PQ), where code values map to intended screen luminance. It is the output of an ODT/Output Transform — what you see and deliver, not the space you do grading or compositing maths in.

The senior on-set technical and creative role owning the digital image pipeline: media offload and checksum verification, on-set colour management (applying LUTs and CDLs), signal monitoring, and exposure/look collaboration with the DOP, plus dailies prep. A DIT can do a data wrangler's job, but not the reverse.

A master Key Delivery Message that does not target a single playback device but instead authorises a trusted facility to generate per-cinema KDMs for an encrypted DCP. It is how a distributor delegates key issuance without ever exposing the underlying content keys in the clear.

Technology and policy that detects and blocks sensitive content from leaving an organisation by unauthorised channels: email, USB, cloud upload, print. In post, DLP plus disabled or blocked removable media are standard TPN-aligned controls to stop assets walking out of the facility.

Dialogue, Music & Effects — the full mix delivered as three separate, time-aligned stem groups that sum back to it. The standard split-track deliverable that lets distributors and dubbing houses re-balance or replace any element; the building block for an M&E.

Digital Matte Painting — a photoreal painted or photo-composited image used to create environments, set extensions or backgrounds that would be impractical to build or shoot. Often projected onto simple geometry ('camera projection') to allow limited camera moves.

Object-based immersive audio format that places sounds as positional objects in 3D space (plus channel-based beds), rendered to the room's actual speaker layout at playback rather than to fixed channels. Adds height; theatrical and home/streaming variants share one authoring model.

Object-based immersive audio deliverable carried as an ADM BWF (Audio Definition Model Broadcast Wave) at 48 kHz / 24-bit, with a binaural re-render for headphones. In cinema, Atmos travels as a separate IAB MXF inside the DCP.

Dolby's proprietary HDR format using PQ with frame-accurate dynamic metadata and up to 12-bit, mastered on calibrated displays (commonly 1,000 or 4,000 nits). The dynamic metadata carries the colourist's intent so it can be tone-mapped down to any consumer screen. Requires licensing.

Delivery configurations of a Dolby Vision stream. Profile 8.1 = HDR10 base + DV RPU; Profile 8.4 = HLG base + DV RPU (both single-layer streaming profiles (base layer is HDR10 / HLG, with a DV RPU metadata sidecar; contrast the dual-layer Profile 7 on UHD Blu-ray)); Profile 5 is single-layer 10-bit (Dolby's proprietary IPT-PQ-c2 colourspace, not HDR10-compatible) (Apple TV+, legacy UHD Blu-ray).

The mezzanine sidecar carrying Dolby Vision analysis/trim metadata, organised in levels: L1 (per-frame source analysis), L2 (trim per target), L6 (carries MaxCLL and MaxFALL). It must be present and frame-accurate to picture or QC rejects it.
Day Out of Days — a scheduling document showing which cast and crew work on which days. Used in the rebate audit to align time records to invoice periods.

The head of the camera and lighting department who authors the visual look of the production and signs off the final grade. In post they collaborate with the colourist to realise on-set intent, often via reference stills and show LUTs established during the shoot.

Digital Picture Exchange (SMPTE ST 268): an uncompressed 10/16-bit image-sequence format, one file per frame, used for legacy VFX, archival image masters and film scans (e.g. 4K log DPX off a scanner).

Encryption-plus-licensing systems that control playback of protected media on approved devices. The three main browser/OTT systems are Google Widevine, Apple FairPlay and Microsoft PlayReady; review and screener platforms layer DRM with forensic watermarking for pre-release content.

A 29.97 fps timecode counting method that skips two frame numbers every minute except minutes divisible by ten (dropping 18 numbers per 10 minutes), keeping timecode aligned to real clock time. No actual frames are dropped, only labels, so durations match broadcast wall-clock.

A momentary loss or corruption of picture or audio (black/white flashes, a torn line, a missing frame, or an audio glitch) caused by media damage, a transfer error, or a bad encode. Automated QC flags each with a timecode for repair.

A sensor with two hardware base sensitivities (e.g. ARRI Alexa 35 at EI 800/2560, Sony VENICE at 800/3200) selectable to optimise noise and dynamic range, rather than gaining a single base ISO up or down. Choosing the correct base avoids unnecessary noise in low light.

A calibrated theatrical-scale mixing room used for the final mix of films, monitored at a fixed reference level (85 dB SPL C-weighted per channel for theatrical, per SMPTE RP 200) over a screen-format speaker system. Contrast with near-field, which mixes on smaller speakers at a lower SPL for home/streaming.

The three core soundtrack categories: DX = dialogue, MX = music, FX = sound effects. Each is edited, premixed and mixed as a separate world before being balanced together, and delivered separately so the mix can be re-balanced or localised.

HDR metadata that updates per scene or per frame, letting the display optimise tone-mapping shot by shot. Used by Dolby Vision (ST 2094-10) and HDR10+ (ST 2094-40); it delivers better results than static metadata on screens dimmer than the mastering display.

The span of brightness a sensor captures from clipped highlight to noise floor, measured in stops (each stop is a doubling of light). Modern cinema cameras claim roughly 14-17 stops (ARRI ALEXA 35: 17); it sets highlight retention and shadow detail and is the headline spec for HDR-friendly capture.

Errors & Omissions insurance, the policy covering a production against claims of copyright/trademark infringement, defamation, invasion of privacy and unauthorised use of names, likenesses or music. Distributors and broadcasters require it before release, and underwriters scrutinise the chain of title, clearances and music cue sheet.

The European 100/0/100/0 vertical colour-bar test pattern (eight full-height bars) used to line up and verify a signal path. It is the EBU equivalent of the SMPTE bars, used in PAL/European workflows.

The EBU recommendation for video signal tolerances in digital TV: RGB components must stay within -5% to 105% and luma within -1% to 103%, with an out-of-gamut error flagged only when the offending area exceeds about 1% of the active picture. It is the European reference for legal-level QC.

The European loudness-normalisation standard: integrated programme loudness targets -23 LUFS (tolerance ±0.5 LU for non-live/file-based, ±1.0 LU for live) with a maximum true peak of -1 dBTP. It measures loudness over the whole programme using ITU-R BS.1770, ending per-channel level juggling. The default outside the US.

The European Broadcasting Union's TTML-based subtitle format for IP distribution (the 'D' is for Distribution). It is one of the two profiles (with SMPTE-TT) that IMSC harmonises, and is widely used by European broadcasters and streamers.

The standard chemical development process for colour motion-picture camera negative. NZ has no commercial photochemical lab for production negative, so exposed neg is couriered overseas (FotoKem, Cinelab) for ECN-2 processing and scanning.

A plain-text list of edit events — each with a reel name, source and record timecodes, and a transition type — used to conform a cut on another system. The dominant flavour is CMX3600, a legacy format limited to one video track, 8-character reel names and basic dissolves/wipes, so complex timelines usually move via AAF or XML instead.

Endpoint security controls required by the CSBP. EDR (Endpoint Detection and Response) monitors and responds to threats on workstations; DLP (Data Loss Prevention) blocks unauthorised data exfiltration. Both are part of the locked-down environment a vetted post vendor must run.

A discrete piece of imagery composited into a shot — practical (fire, smoke, water, dust, sparks shot against black) or CG. Often pulled from stock libraries or shot on an 'elements unit' for reuse across multiple shots.

The decode curve in the display that converts incoming code values into emitted light (nits). It is the receiving end of the transfer-function chain: SDR uses a gamma EOTF (BT.1886), while HDR uses PQ (ST 2084) or HLG.

Human visual-and-aural review of every dailies clip for picture and sound faults. Picture checks include focus (the single most critical), strobing/banding, moire/aliasing, vignetting and boom-in-shot; sound checks include dropouts, radio-mic bumps and dialogue interference.

Standard QC verdict tiers: Pass (meets spec), Fail (a hard fault that blocks delivery and must be fixed), and Pass-with-note (a fault that exists but is acceptable or unavoidable, flagged for the client to acknowledge). Exact severity definitions vary by deliverable spec.

The NZ/UK/Commonwealth copyright exception (NZ Copyright Act 1994) permitting limited use of copyright material for specific purposes such as criticism, review, news reporting, research or parody. It is narrower and more rule-based than US 'fair use', so productions should not assume US fair-use latitude in New Zealand.

Apple's DRM (Digital Rights Management) system, used across Safari and Apple devices via HLS (HTTP Live Streaming). It encrypts content with AES-128 in CBCS mode (the SAMPLE-AES variant), and on Apple silicon hardware-backed decryption via the Secure Enclave keeps decoded frames out of normal memory to resist screen capture.

A monitoring overlay that maps exposure values to flat colours (e.g. red for clipping, purple for crushed blacks, green/grey for mid-tones) so the operator can set exposure precisely. Standard on-set tool for DITs and focus pullers judging skin tone and highlight clipping.

XML-based timeline interchange formats. 'Legacy XML' (XMEML) comes from Final Cut Pro 7 and is widely read by Resolve, Premiere and others; FCPXML is Final Cut Pro X's structurally different, resource-and-storyline-based format (now an .fcpxmld bundle). Both carry richer effect and clip data than an EDL but vary in how faithfully each app translates them.

An open ASC specification (v1.0 released April 2023) that records framing intent — the canvas, framing intents and crop/protect areas chosen by the DOP — in a portable JSON sidecar or embedded in camera files, so framing survives accurately from camera through VFX and finishing. It decouples the creative frame from the recorded sensor area.

For interlaced video, whether the upper (top) or lower (bottom) field is displayed first. A wrong field-order flag makes motion stutter or tear backwards, and is a frequent automated-QC and conform fault.

Digitising processed negative to image files (typically 4K log DPX) on a film scanner such as ARRISCAN XT or Lasergraphics Director. NZ shoots usually process and scan at the same overseas lab; Park Road Post can scan-only in NZ for archival or colourist-supervised work.

A shot signed off by the VFX supervisor and director/client as finished, cleared to replace its temp in the conform and master. Editorial flips its status from temp to final and logs the approved version for delivery.

The re-recording mixer balancing dialogue, music and effects to picture against the creative intent and the deliverable's loudness/peak spec, on a dub stage or in the box. The output becomes the printmaster and the stems.
Producer responsible for the colour-and-online finishing stage, reporting to the Post Supervisor. Coordinates grade and online suite scheduling and deliverable output.

The distance (in mm) from a lens's optical centre to the sensor when focused at infinity, determining field of view and apparent magnification — shorter is wider, longer is tighter. On a given sensor it sets how much of the scene fits the frame.

The 1st Assistant Camera (1st AC), responsible for maintaining critical focus during takes, marking actors and lens changes, and managing the camera and lenses. The crew member who keeps the image sharp shot to shot.

Custom sound effects performed live in sync to picture by a foley artist on a foley stage — footsteps, cloth movement, prop handling. Built fresh rather than pulled from libraries because canned effects rarely land in sync; named after Jack Foley. Essential for fully-filled M&E so dubbed versions are not hollow.
Subtitles that must always appear regardless of the viewer's language setting — for foreign dialogue, on-screen signage or text — delivered burned-in or as an IMSC track carrying a forced flag.

Embedding an invisible, recipient-unique identifier into picture or audio so a leaked copy can be traced back to whoever it was issued to. It is designed to survive re-encoding, cropping and camcording, unlike a visible burn-in, and is common in cinema playout (DCI servers), screeners and high-value review streams.

The Burbank (Los Angeles) film lab that is the default for most NZ 35mm shoots: ECN-2 processing, 4K/6K scanning, best-light dailies and archival scans. Neg flies AKL→LAX overnight, is processed and scanned the same LA day, and files return to NZ next morning (roughly 36-48 hr dailies on 35mm).

A retiming method that cross-dissolves adjacent source frames to fill the gaps when slowing or speeding footage. Cheaper and more predictable than optical flow but visibly softer and ghosted, so it's the fallback when optical flow breaks down.

In TV drama, '24fps' almost always means 23.976 fps (24000/1001), the NTSC-derived fractional rate that is the global default for Netflix/Amazon originals. True 24.000 fps is reserved for some cinema work and DCPs. Labelling a 23.976 master as 24.000 causes progressive sync drift across a feature.

Changing a clip's frame rate. Interpolation (synthesising in-between frames) is banned for delivery masters; mixing rates is resolved by a discrete conform or a whole-programme speed/slow (e.g. 24 to 25 for PAL), never by altering motion.

Adobe-owned cloud review and Camera-to-Cloud platform. Proxies upload with the CDL/ODT baked in (not colour-managed). Strong Premiere integration and a broad C2C device ecosystem; HDR support is limited.

Automated checks that flag unintended frozen frames, full-black frames, or audio silence beyond a set duration. These are common signs of an export error, a gap in the timeline, or a missing audio stem, and are standard items in any QC template.
A production and VFX shot-tracking and review platform used as an alternative to ShotGrid for daily and weekly work-in-progress review on VFX shows.

Sensors at or beyond the 36x24mm stills frame. "Full Frame" (FF) sits at that 35mm-stills size (e.g. Sony VENICE at about 36x24mm); "Large Format" (LF) is the umbrella term for FF and larger (e.g. ARRI ALEXA 65). Bigger sensors widen field of view and shallow the depth of field for a given framing, and demand wider-covering glass.
The chief lighting technician on set, who designs and executes the lighting plan under the DOP's direction and runs the electrical/lighting crew. Their second-in-charge is the Best Boy Electric.

The fixed power-law transfer curve used for DCI-P3 digital cinema projection, paired with a 48 cd/m2 reference white in a fully dark theatre. It is steeper than the 2.4 used for SDR TV because cinema viewing is darker.

A non-linear transfer function applied to image data to suit display response and use bit depth efficiently. The transfer characteristic (gamma curve) must be tagged correctly in the file or QC fails.

The full range of colours a device or colour space can reproduce, plotted as a triangle on the CIE chromaticity diagram whose corners are the red, green and blue primaries. A wider gamut (more saturated primaries) encloses more visible colour.

A signal whose RGB components fall outside the legal range (too negative or too hot) even if luma/chroma look fine — typically created by aggressive colour grading. It is spotted on a Diamond or dedicated gamut display and flagged when the offending area is significant (per EBU R103).

Reconciling colours from a wider source gamut into a smaller one without ugly hue shifts or clipping. ACES ships a reference Gamut Compression Look Transform (LMT) to tame extreme out-of-gamut values (often negative channels from saturated lights or lasers), and ACES 2.0's Output Transforms include far more robust gamut handling.

The EU General Data Protection Regulation. It can apply to a NZ production that handles personal data of people in the EU or works with EU partners; its consent, breach-notification and cross-border transfer obligations are stricter than the NZ Privacy Act, so productions selling into Europe must account for it.

The running log the Post Supervisor keeps on each show recording every generative-AI tool, vendor, use case and approval status. Vendors must disclose Gen-AI use in their SOW (Statement of Work), and enterprise-only tools are enforced when production data is involved.

The Netflix form producers must complete for higher-risk generative-AI uses: any final on-screen output, altering talent likeness or voice (de-ageing, deepfakes), use on protected/unreleased material, use of public (non-enterprise) tools, or any model trained on production material.

aka: Using Generative AI in Content Production
definition: Netflix's 2025 guidance setting five principles for generative AI: no regurgitation of copyrighted material, no displacement of talent/crafts, Gen-AI output temporary by default, keep inputs and outputs in secured enterprise environments, and disclose and get approval. A Gen AI Use Case Questionnaire is required for flagged higher-risk uses.

Generator Locking — feeding a common reference sync signal (tri-level or black burst) to multiple cameras/devices so their frames and timecode align for multi-camera, LED-volume or live workflows. Without genlock, sources drift and cannot be cut or composited frame-accurately.
Storing copies in two or more physically separate locations, at least one offsite, so a localised disaster cannot destroy every copy. A hard requirement for Netflix and Amazon originals.

A sensor readout that exposes all pixels at the same instant, eliminating rolling-shutter skew and flash banding — important for fast action, strobes and LED walls. Found on cameras such as the RED KOMODO and Sony BURANO/VENICE 2 (in some modes).

The ordered set of derivations between masters — e.g. a Dolby Vision HDR master and its mapped lower-nit trims, an HDR-to-SDR Rec.709 down-trim, and a P3-D65 DCP to Rec.709 re-grade. Always grade HDR first; retrofitting HDR from an SDR grade is not recommended.

Reference spheres shot on set: the chrome (mirror) ball captures the lighting environment and reflections, while the grey (18% diffuse) ball shows light direction, colour and exposure. Together they let lighting artists match CG to the plate.

Crew responsible for camera support and rigging — dollies, cranes, mounts — and the non-electrical shaping of light (flags, nets, diffusion frames). Led by the Key Grip; distinct from the electrical (gaffer/sparks) department.
Long-GOP (inter-frame) distribution codec in MP4/MOV/MXF. Used for streaming, proxies, and cloud-review reference files — not a delivery-master codec.

Extra frames included beyond the cut points at the head and tail of a clip when pulling media for VFX or conform, giving downstream artists room to slip, retime or extend a shot. 8 frames each side is a common editorial default, though the number is agreed per project and per vendor.

High-bandwidth Digital Content Protection — link encryption on HDMI/DisplayPort that stops protected video being captured between source and display. HDCP 2.2 or higher is generally mandated for 4K/UHD premium content; a non-compliant monitor or capture card anywhere in the chain will down-res or black the signal.

Imaging that delivers a much wider range from deep blacks to bright highlights than SDR, usually paired with a wide colour gamut, using the PQ or HLG transfer functions standardised in ITU-R BT.2100. It enables specular highlights well above the ~100-nit SDR ceiling for a more lifelike picture.

A colourist-adjusted set of metadata telling an HDR system how to render the HDR master on a lower-capability display (e.g. a 100-nit SDR screen or a 600-nit TV), preserving creative intent rather than relying on automatic tone-mapping. A trim pass is required for Dolby Vision delivery.

The open, royalty-free baseline HDR format: PQ transfer, Rec.2020 container, 10-bit, with static (whole-programme) metadata only — ST 2086 mastering-display data plus MaxCLL and MaxFALL. Commonly mastered to a 1,000–4,000 nit peak. The most widely supported HDR format.

High Dynamic Range Image — a 360-degree, high-dynamic-range capture of the set lighting taken on the day (via a chrome/grey ball or panoramic rig), used by VFX to light and reflect CG so it matches the photography. A core on-set data-capture deliverable for the VFX team.
Long-GOP distribution codec succeeding H.264, in MP4/MOV/MXF. Used for 4K/HDR streaming and UHD Blu-ray (Main 10 profile, 10-bit).

Capture or projection above the 24fps norm. As an in-camera tool, high frame rates (e.g. 60-120fps+) are conformed back to project speed for slow motion; as a delivery format (e.g. 48/60fps projection) HFR reduces motion judder. Always confirm whether HFR means a slow-mo capture or a true high-rate deliverable.

A scene-referred, relative HDR transfer function jointly developed by the BBC and NHK and standardised in ITU-R BT.2100, combining a gamma curve in the shadows with a log curve in the highlights. Its key advantage is reasonable backward compatibility on SDR screens, making it the broadcast-friendly HDR format; nominal peak is around 1,000 cd/m2 with a display-dependent system gamma.

A matte (or deep-image occlusion) that stops one element drawing over another — e.g. a CG creature held out where a foreground actor crosses in front, avoiding visible overlap. In deep compositing, holdouts resolve automatically from per-pixel depth.

Intra-coded frame — a fully self-contained compressed frame that needs no other frames to decode, used as the random-access/edit point in GOP-based codecs. I-frame-only codecs (e.g. ProRes, DNxHR, AVC-Intra) compress each frame independently for editing.

Immersive Audio Bitstream (SMPTE ST 2098-2): the cinema carriage for object-based audio (Dolby Atmos) in a DCP — a separate IAB MXF rendered from the print master, played back in IAB-capable auditoriums.

Colours that exceed broadcast-legal limits — out-of-gamut RGB or out-of-range luma/chroma — which can cause clipping, blooming or audio buzz on transmission. Fixed with a legaliser or by regrading, though legalisers can themselves introduce visible artefacts.

The diameter of the circular image a lens projects at the focal plane. It must equal or exceed the sensor's diagonal or the corners go dark (vignette); a Super 35 lens on a full-frame open-gate sensor typically will not cover. This is the first check when pairing legacy glass with large-format bodies.

A component-based, file-based master format standardised in the SMPTE ST 2067 suite, holding one programme as a set of reusable track files (video, audio, subtitles) plus playlists that assemble different versions without duplicating media. Built for global distribution: edit/language/territory variants ship as lightweight supplemental packages rather than full re-renders.

The actual deliverable folder of an IMF: one or more MXF track files plus the XML sidecars (CPL, PKL, ASSETMAP, optional OPL) that describe and assemble them. An IMP is either an OV (complete, self-contained) or a Supplemental/VF that references assets in another package.

aka: TTML Profiles for Internet Media Subtitles and Captions
definition: Internet Media Subtitles and Captions — a W3C profile of TTML that unifies SMPTE-TT and EBU-TT-D into one interchange format for subtitles/captions, and is the current professional and streaming standard (IMSC 1.1 builds on TTML2). It carries text, timing and styling and is compatible with MP4 and MXF wrappers.

The transform that converts a specific camera's native encoding (e.g. ARRI LogC, Sony S-Log3/S-Gamut3, RED Log3G10/REDWideGamutRGB) into scene-linear ACES2065-1. 'Input Device Transform' was the original term; from ACES 1.0 the preferred name is simply 'Input Transform'.

A scan type that draws each frame as two fields (alternate lines). 50i = 50 fields/sec = 25 frames interlaced; 1080i50 is TVNZ's traditional broadcast format. Interlacing artefacts (combing) are a QC flag on progressive deliverables.

Interlaced (i) draws each frame as two fields of alternate lines (odd then even); progressive (p) draws every line in one pass. Interlace doubles temporal sampling for a given bandwidth but causes combing on motion; progressive is cleaner and standard for digital cinema and streaming.

The international stream of the NZ rebate aimed at foreign productions and PDV (Post, Digital and Visual effects) work: a 20% base on QNZPE plus a 5% uplift for productions meeting significant economic-benefit criteria. From January 2026 the PDV-specific uplift and a lowered NZ$20 million uplift threshold (down from NZ$30 million) make NZ post and VFX work more competitive.
The legacy DCP interoperability standard predating SMPTE DCP, now retired by most major chains. Mixing an InterOp KDM with a SMPTE DCP (or vice versa) is a common KDM failure.

Two construction standards for a Digital Cinema Package. InterOp (IOP) is the older pre-standard format, effectively limited to 24/48 fps and a narrower feature set but very widely compatible with older servers. SMPTE DCP (governed by SMPTE ST 429-2) is the current standard, supporting 24-120 fps, closed captions and more, but is not played by InterOp-only servers; the package name carries IOP or SMPTE so projectionists know which they have.

Removing 3:2 pulldown to recover the original 23.976/24p progressive frames from interlaced 29.97 video, by detecting the cadence and discarding the duplicated fields. Essential before reframing or speed changes to avoid combing.

The canonical DCP filename standard (from the Inter-Society Digital Cinema Forum) that cinema servers parse for metadata, e.g. TITLE_FTR-1_F-185_EN-XX_INT-TD_51_4K_STUDIO_DATE_FACILITY_OV: title, content kind, format (F-185=1.85 Flat, F-239=2.39 Scope, F-178=1.78/16:9), language-subs, territory-rating, audio config, resolution, studio, date, facility, OV/VF. A wrong name is a leading cause of ingest failure.

The international standard for an ISMS (Information Security Management System), 2022 edition. It defines how an organisation manages security risk through documented controls; the MPA Content Security Best Practices map to it, so 27001 certification eases TPN (Trusted Partner Network) assessment.

The base ITU recommendation defining how to measure programme loudness (K-weighting filter; gated mean with a -70 LKFS absolute and -10 LU relative gate) and true-peak level. Every loudness spec — EBU R128, ATSC A/85, Netflix — is built on it; revisions (-1 through -5) refine the gating and true-peak method.

Split edits where audio and picture cut at different points: in a J-cut the next scene's audio leads the picture; in an L-cut the outgoing audio lingers over the new picture. Standard technique for smooth, motivated scene transitions.

The wavelet-based, intra-frame image codec mandated for DCP picture essence, where every frame is compressed independently for precise quality control and frame-accurate access. The DCI maximum bit rate for cinema is 250 Mbit/s (raised to 500 Mbit/s for HDR/Dolby Vision and high-frame-rate content).

The encrypted message delivering the AES content keys needed to play an encrypted DCP (or IMF) on one specific media block, valid only for a defined date/time window and carrying the forensic-marking instructions. It authorises a cinema or screening room to play that film, and only that film, on that server, for that engagement.

Generating a matte from colour — pulling a key off a chroma backing (green or blue) to separate foreground from background. Green suits digital sensors because Bayer patterns carry twice as many green photosites, giving the cleanest, least-noisy channel; blue is used for night scenes or when green appears in costume/props.

The report produced during dailies/transfer documenting how camera originals were processed into editorial media — applied LUT/CDL, sync notes, transcode settings, technical issues flagged and the files generated. It establishes what the editor is actually cutting with versus the camera original.
A small clip-on personal microphone worn on or hidden in clothing to capture an individual's dialogue, usually via a bodypack radio transmitter. The workhorse of dialogue capture alongside the boom.

Whether code values use the broadcast-legal window (8-bit 16–235 / 10-bit 64–940, 'limited/narrow') or the full range (8-bit 0–255 / 10-bit 0–1023). Broadcast is always legal/limited range; full range only when a spec demands it. A mismatch crushes blacks or clips whites.

Real lenses bend straight lines (barrel/pincushion distortion). The plate is 'undistorted' to a rectilinear state before tracking and CG work, then the same distortion is re-applied ('redistort') to the comp so CG and plate edges align. A distortion grid shot on set calibrates the model.

The mechanical and electronic interface between lens and body, defining flange depth (mount-to-sensor distance) plus data and control. PL (Positive Lock) is the long-standing cinema standard; LPL (Large-format PL) suits big sensors with a shorter flange; EF, E and RF cross over from stills. Mount and flange depth dictate which glass physically fits and covers the sensor.

The Dolby M-weighted equivalent-loudness measure used to police theatrical trailers and ads (the common ceiling is Leq(m) 85). It pairs with the cinema reference where a -20 dBFS RMS pink-noise signal calibrates each screen channel to 85 dB SPL (SMPTE RP 200), so dialogue typically sits around -31 dBFS RMS at reference level.

Low Frequency Effects — the dedicated low-bass '.1' channel in 5.1/7.1 (and Atmos bed) feeding the subwoofer, band-limited to roughly 120 Hz and carrying impact/rumble. It is a discrete effects channel, not a derived bass sum from the main speakers.

Light Detection And Ranging — a laser scanner that captures a precise 3D point cloud of a set or location for accurate match-move, set extensions and digital environments. Often paired with photogrammetry for textured geometry.

The three primary tonal controls in a grade: lift moves shadows/black point, gamma moves mid-tones, gain moves highlights/white point — each adjustable per RGB channel for colour balance. The bedrock primary controls underlying ASC-CDL's slope/offset/power.

A colour encoding where pixel values are directly proportional to real-world light, so compositing operations (blur, blend, defocus) behave physically correctly. It is the mandatory working state for VFX and CG rendering, distinct from the display-referred curves used for viewing.

A lineup confirms a turned-over plate matches its intended shot — frame ranges, handles and slate checked against the cut reference. The shotlist (or VFX list) is the master register of every VFX shot: number, description, vendor, status and complexity.

Pomfort's on-set DIT grading and CDL-management application. Runs the full on-set ACES pipeline (IDT to CDL to ODT), stores a CDL per shot, broadcasts looks to connected devices, and exports an end-of-day ALE for the lab.

An ACES-to-ACES transform that applies a deliberate, image-wide creative 'look' (e.g. a show LUT or print emulation) which is then seen through the Output Transform, all without leaving the ACES pipeline. 'Look Transform' is the current preferred term; LMT is the legacy acronym still in common use.

A signed permission from a property owner or controller allowing filming at a site and use of the resulting footage, including any visible signage, artwork or trademarks. It is part of clearance and the chain of title.

The specific approved version of the timeline frozen at picture lock and used as the single source of truth for all downstream turnovers. Any deviation from it must be communicated as a documented change so sound, VFX and DI stay in sync.

A camera gamma curve that stores sensor data logarithmically to preserve the full captured dynamic range in a limited bit depth, producing the flat, low-contrast image that holds maximum highlight and shadow detail for grading. Each manufacturer has its own flavour (ARRI LogC, Sony S-Log3, Panasonic V-Log, RED Log3G10), so the correct input transform must be matched per camera.

A logarithmic transfer function that maps the sensor's wide linear dynamic range into a limited-bit recording, the way film density responds to light — prioritising tonal range over a display-ready look. Log footage looks flat and desaturated until a LUT or grade is applied, and it is not a colour space on its own (it pairs with a gamut).

ARRI's log transfer curve, paired with ARRI Wide Gamut. Log-C3 is the long-standing version; Log-C4 ships on the Alexa 35. Each version needs its matching IDT (Input Device Transform) in an ACES pipeline.

RED's IPP2 working encoding. Log3G10 maps 18% mid-grey to one-third (0.333) of the maximum code value and encodes up to 10 stops above mid-grey; REDWideGamutRGB is a camera gamut designed to contain every colour any RED sensor can capture without clipping.

ARRI's log curves. LogC3 (Alexa Classic through Mini LF) is Exposure-Index-dependent; LogC4, built for the ALEXA 35's ALEV4 sensor, is EI-independent and places middle grey at a lower code value to hold the extra highlight headroom across roughly 17 stops. Each pairs with an ARRI Wide Gamut colour space.

A codec structure that compresses a Group Of Pictures using predicted (P) and bidirectional (B) frames between I-frames, giving small files at the cost of edit/seek complexity. Common for acquisition and delivery (XDCAM, H.264) but heavier to edit than intra-frame.

aka: Loudness K-weighted relative to Full Scale
Loudness Units relative to Full Scale
definition: Integrated programme loudness measured per ITU-R BS.1770 (LKFS and LUFS are identical units; 1 LU = 1 dB). US/Canada broadcast (ATSC A/85) targets -24 LKFS; Europe (EBU R128) targets -23 LUFS; most streamers normalise around -23 to -24. Getting it wrong is one of the most common QC rejections.

A BS.1770/EBU statistic, measured in LU, describing the spread of loudness across a programme (the dynamic variation between its quiet and loud passages). High LRA can mean dialogue gets buried; very low LRA means an over-compressed, flat mix.
Linear Tape File System. A file system for LTO tape that lets the tape be mounted and browsed like a drive instead of read sequentially. It is the industry-default way to store and retrieve from LTO.

Linear Tape-Open. Magnetic data-tape format for archival storage; native uncompressed capacities are LTO-7 = 6 TB, LTO-8 = 12 TB, LTO-9 = 18 TB. Master and clone tapes are kept in two physically separate secure locations.

In a Y'CbCr signal, luma (Y') is the weighted brightness channel and chroma (Cb, Cr) are the two colour-difference channels. Separating them lets chroma be subsampled without visibly hurting the image, since the eye resolves brightness more sharply than colour.

A pre-computed table that maps input colour values to output values, used to apply a camera-to-display conversion or a creative look on set, in dailies and in the grade. A 3D LUT (e.g. a .cube file) remaps the full colour cube; a 1D LUT adjusts each channel independently.

Music & Effects: the complete soundtrack minus production dialogue and narration, used to create foreign-language dubs. It must be 'fully-filled' — every spot that carried original dialogue needs replacement foley/FX so the dubbed mix is not hollow — and the music must be cleared for international use.

Blocky artefacts at the boundaries of compression macroblocks, visible as false squares or tiling when bitrate is too low or a file is over-compressed. A core metric for automated QC; also called tiling, mosaicing or pixelation.
Software that catalogues, searches and manages media assets in a cloud or facility (e.g. Iconik, MediaValet, SonyCi, Bynder). The searchable backbone of a cloud-native or MovieLabs-aligned pipeline.

Permission from the owner of a specific sound recording (usually the record label) to use that exact recording in your project. It is distinct from the sync licence on the underlying song: using a commercial track in picture needs both, from two different rights-holders.

The calibrated reference monitor on which an HDR grade is created (commonly a 1,000- or 4,000-nit, P3-in-2020 display such as a Sony BVM-HX310 or Dolby PRM). Its colour volume is recorded in ST 2086 metadata so downstream displays know the creative reference conditions.

HDR static metadata (SMPTE ST 2086) describing the grading monitor used — its colour primaries and peak luminance (e.g. 1,000-nit P3-D65) — so downstream displays know the reference the master was approved on.

An edit command that locates and loads the exact source frame under the playhead back into the source viewer, letting an editor trace a cut back to its master clip or extend a shot. A core navigation tool when conforming or reworking a sequence.

A 3x3 linear operation that converts between RGB gamuts (and to/from CIE XYZ) by mixing the channels — the precise way to move between, say, AP0 and AP1 primaries. In a CLF or LUT chain the matrix handles the gamut/primary change while 1D LUTs handle the transfer curve.
The metadata flag defining the RGB-to-YCbCr conversion maths. HDR masters typically use Rec.2020 non-constant luminance (NCL); mis-tagging it causes colour errors that QC will flag.

A greyscale image defining which parts of a frame are visible or transparent — white passes, black blocks, grey is partial. Used to isolate regions for compositing; an alpha channel is simply a matte travelling embedded with an image.

Maximum Content Light Level. HDR static-metadata value giving the brightest single pixel (in nits) anywhere in the programme. It must be measured from the final graded master with a validator, never estimated; Netflix defaults to a 1,000-nit target.

HDR10 dynamic light-level metadata: MaxCLL (Maximum Content Light Level) is the brightest single pixel in the whole programme, in nits; MaxFALL (Maximum Frame-Average Light Level) is the highest frame-average brightness. QC checks these match the content and the deliverable spec (e.g. a 1000-nit master).

Maximum Frame-Average Light Level. The highest frame-average brightness across the whole programme, in nits. Paired with MaxCLL as static HDR metadata, it protects displays from sustained full-screen brightness.

The discipline of organising, consolidating, copying, trimming and tracking media and metadata across a project so files stay findable, relinkable and backed up. Covers tasks like consolidating to a single drive, trimming media to used ranges (plus handles), and keeping reel/clip naming consistent.

Non-image data embedded per clip or frame: timecode, reel/roll, ISO/EI, white balance, lens, focus/iris/zoom (via protocols like ARRI LDS or Cooke /i Technology), shutter, frame rate and look (CDL/LUT). It carries through to dailies, editorial and VFX, so accurate on-set metadata saves time downstream.

The high-quality master a platform keeps and re-encodes all downstream versions from. It must be good enough that any future codec or HDR format can be derived from it. Common forms: IMF, ProRes 4444 XQ, DNxHR 444/HQX, JPEG 2000 MXF, DPX, OpenEXR.

Multi-Factor Authentication — requiring two or more independent proofs of identity (password plus a phone code, hardware key or biometric) before granting access. A baseline control mandated by MPA/TPN content-security programmes for systems holding pre-release media.
A music-licensing contract clause guaranteeing each licensor parity: if one licensor negotiates a better deal, all MFN licensors automatically get the same terms.
Interference patterns (moire) or jagged stair-stepped edges (aliasing) produced when fine repeating detail exceeds what the sensor or raster can resolve. Checked during dailies eyeball QC.

The directional smearing of moving subjects recorded while the shutter is open, governed by shutter angle and frame rate. The 180-degree shutter "film look" is the reference; too little blur reads as strobing/judder, too much as dreamy smear. Matters for VFX match-moving and frame-rate conversion.

Motion Picture Laboratories, a non-profit R&D joint venture of the major studios. It publishes the '2030 Vision' for cloud-native production and the security architecture (CSAP) underpinning it, and shares overlapping studio membership with TPN.

The industry reference architecture for cloud-native, software-defined, security-first production. Its ten principles include assets created/ingested straight into the cloud, applications coming to the media (not vice versa), active searchable archives, intrinsic per-asset security, strong federated identity, ubiquitous machine-readable metadata, and real-time collaboration as the default.

Motion Picture Association, the trade body of the major US studios (Disney, Universal, Sony, Paramount, Warner Bros. Discovery and Netflix). It owns and operates the Trusted Partner Network (TPN) and authors the Content Security Best Practices most studio post and VFX vendors are contractually held to.

The MPA's media-and-entertainment-specific information-security control framework (CSBP), the basis of TPN assessments. The current version is mapped to ISO/IEC 27001:2022, ISO/IEC 27002:2022, NIST SP 800-53 Rev 5, CSA Cloud Controls Matrix v4 and AICPA Trust Services Criteria, and covers facility, application, cloud and software-development controls.

The dominant content-security vetting programme for facilities that handle studio/streamer media, owned by the Motion Picture Association (MPA). Its next-generation Shield tiers (rolled out from 2025 with the v5.3.1 questionnaire) run Blue (self-assessed and published), Silver (assessed with a remediation plan, the common studio minimum, formerly Classic Gold), Gold (all best-practice items remediated and TPN-reviewed), and Gold Star (best practice plus additional recommendations completed). Legacy 'Classic' Gold Shields remain valid until expiry.

An editing mode that syncs multiple camera angles (by timecode, audio or markers) into one grouped clip so the editor can cut live between angles in real time. Essential for concert, panel, sitcom and event coverage.
