An EXR holding many render passes in one file. 'Multichannel' packs extra channels/layers (beauty, AOVs, IDs) into a single image, while 'multipart' (added in OpenEXR 2.0) stores separate independent parts so a comp can load only the channels it needs, speeding reads.
Clears music for the production (sync and master-use rights). Distinct from the Post Supervisor, who ensures the resulting cue sheet is filed and the licences are reflected in the delivery pack.

The professional media wrapper standardised as SMPTE ST 377-1 that bundles video/audio essence with timecode and metadata in one container, independent of the codec inside. The same wrapper carries DCP, IMF and broadcast deliverables; its behaviour is set by its Operational Pattern (OP1a, OP-Atom, etc.).
Non-Aligned Materials card — a Netflix delivery element accompanying the branded master, supplied alongside end-card templates and other house elements specified in the title plan.

The frame rate the material was shot at, which must be preserved exactly through to delivery. Netflix, Amazon and all major platforms prohibit frame-rate conversion: a motion-interpolated or upconverted master is rejected.

Native ISO is the sensitivity at which the sensor delivers its optimal, balanced dynamic range (e.g. ARRI 800; VENICE dual-base 800/3200). Exposure Index (EI) is the rating you choose to shoot at; on most cinema cameras EI re-balances how stops are distributed above and below grey without changing the underlying RAW capture.

A grey filter that cuts light evenly across all colours so you can hold a wide aperture or 180-degree shutter in bright conditions. Strength is given as optical density: 0.3 = 1 stop, 0.6 = 2 stops, 0.9 = 3 stops (each 0.3 is one stop). IRND variants also block infrared to prevent the red contamination dense ND can cause on digital sensors; many cinema cameras have motorised internal ND.

A confidentiality contract binding cast, crew, vendors and viewers not to disclose plot, footage, assets or production information. Routinely required before access to scripts, dailies or screeners on studio and streamer projects; breach can carry significant liquidated damages.

Two monitoring contexts for mixing. A dub stage is a large calibrated theatre at 85 dB SPL reference for cinema; near-field uses smaller speakers close to the mixer at a lower SPL (commonly ~76–82 dB), matching how home/streaming audiences listen. Streaming platforms often require a separate near-field mix.

A separate insurance rider (often underwritten at Lloyd's of London) covering exposed negative from the DIT cart through to the lab and back, since standard production insurance frequently lapses once neg is handed to a freight forwarder. Essential on any 35mm shoot — the declared value equals the re-shoot cost.

Netflix's master-delivery package: IMF (SMPTE ST 2067-21 App 2E, preferred) or ProRes 4444 XQ; UHD or HD at native frame rate (no conversions); SDR Rec.709/BT.1886 or HDR Rec.2020 PQ — originals delivering in HDR must be mastered in Dolby Vision (Profile 8.1), from which HDR10 and SDR are derived. Audio 48 kHz/24-bit at -27 LKFS dialog-gated, true peak ≤ -2 dBTP; plus textless, proxy, M&E, stems, NAM card, IMSC subtitles and a QC report. Authoritative spec lives at partnerhelp.netflixstudios.com.

Netflix's content-security overlay (the program historically referenced as NP3) layered on top of TPN: typically requires TPN Silver or higher, forensic and unique per-user watermarking on screeners and pre-release playback, a named accountable security contact, and prompt incident reporting (within roughly 24 hours of any suspected exposure).

Netflix specifies an integrated loudness of -27 LKFS (±2 LU), measured dialog-gated with Dolby Dialogue Intelligence so the figure reflects only speech rather than the whole programme, and true peaks at or below -2 dBTP. This differs from programme-gated broadcast specs (R128, A/85) and keeps dialogue intelligibility consistent across titles.

aka: NZ Archive of Film, Television and Sound
definition: New Zealand's national audiovisual archive, responsible for collecting and preserving the country's film, television and sound heritage. It is the standard destination for archival deposit of NZ-funded screen works and a reference for preservation-grade master requirements.

The US NIST (National Institute of Standards and Technology) catalogue of security and privacy controls, Revision 5, used as a baseline across industries. The MPA Content Security Best Practices map to it, so its control families recur in studio security questionnaires.

The unit of display luminance (candela per square metre). Reference points: SDR ~100 nits peak; HDR10 minimum display ~400 nits; grading-reference HDR monitor 1,000 nits; Netflix 1,000-nit MaxCLL target; PQ ceiling 10,000 nits; Dolby Cinema projector ~108 nits (2D).

Timecode that counts every frame sequentially with no skipped numbers; at 29.97 fps it drifts about 3.6 seconds slow per hour versus real time (roughly 1.8 frames per minute). Preferred where frame-count accuracy matters more than clock accuracy.

National Television System Committee: the North American broadcast heritage of fractional rates behind 23.976, 29.97 and 59.94 fps. '30fps' almost always means 29.97; US sports '60fps' is usually 59.94.

New Zealand's government screen agency, which funds and supports NZ films and administers the New Zealand Screen Production Rebate (NZSPR). Its delivery and reporting requirements feed directly into a production's deliverables list and archival obligations.

The Crown funding agency (Irirangi Te Motu) that invests in local public-media content — television, music, podcasts and digital — across NZ platforms. A primary domestic commissioning body whose contracts carry their own delivery and metadata requirements.

The New Zealand International Film Festival (Whānau Mārama), which issues an annual festival tech sheet, ingests DCPs at its tech office, and uses festival-window KDMs (time-limited decryption keys). Festival shorts are often delivered unencrypted to reduce operational friction.

New Zealand's cash-rebate scheme on qualifying local spend (QNZPE), administered by the NZ Film Commission: a 40% Domestic rebate for New Zealand productions (on behalf of Manatū Taonga / Ministry for Culture & Heritage) and a 20% International rebate with available uplifts (policy set by MBIE). From 2026 a 5% PDV (Post, Digital and Visual effects) uplift is available and minimum-spend thresholds were lowered.

Offline is the creative edit cut on lightweight proxy media to reach picture lock; online is the full-resolution rebuild (conform) of that decision list for finishing. Splitting them keeps editorial fast while reserving heavy media handling for the finishing stage.

The creative editing phase done on lightweight proxy/low-resolution media, where story, pacing and structure are decided without the system overhead of full-res footage. The offline produces the locked cut and an edit list (EDL/AAF/XML) that drives the online conform — not the final-quality master itself.

A legacy interchange format, predecessor to AAF, mainly used to hand audio from picture editorial to a DAW with embedded media and basic edit/level data. Largely superseded by AAF (which handles longer sessions and more metadata), but still requested by some sound facilities.

The editor who rebuilds the locked offline edit at full resolution from camera originals, matching it frame-accurately to the offline via the EDL/AAF/XML, then handles versioning, titles, format-specific reframes and the technical master. The bridge between creative editorial and final delivery.

The finishing phase where the offline's edit decisions are rebuilt against the full-resolution camera originals to create the broadcast/delivery master. Online covers conform, relinking to original media, VFX integration, titles and grade, typically in Resolve, Flame, Baselight or Avid Symphony.

Two MXF Operational Patterns. OP1a packs picture and all audio tracks into a single self-contained file on one timeline — the interoperable choice for delivery. OP-Atom puts each track (picture, and each audio channel) in its own separate file — efficient for Avid editing, but you need the complete set of files to reconstruct the program.

Recording the sensor's full photosite area rather than a cropped sub-frame, maximising resolution and leaving room for VFX stabilisation, reframing and multi-format extraction. Open gate often exceeds the delivery aspect ratio, so framing charts and lens coverage must be checked carefully.

The Academy Software Foundation's open-source colour-management library and de facto standard for VFX and animation, driven by a central config.ocio file. The config defines the available colour spaces, roles, displays and views, so every package (Nuke, Maya, Houdini, Resolve, Flame, etc.) shares one consistent ACES pipeline.

The open, high-dynamic-range floating-point image format (from ILM, Industrial Light & Magic) that is the VFX industry standard for rendered frames and plates. It supports multiple channels and layers (AOVs, arbitrary output variables), lossless and lossy compression, and holds scene-linear values far beyond the 0-1 range.

An IMF-specific XML file carrying machine-readable recipe instructions (crop, scale, colour-space conversion, transcode parameters) for deriving a specific deliverable from a CPL. It lets a transcoder generate a target format automatically rather than needing a human to set up the render.

A motion-estimation retiming method that synthesises brand-new in-between frames by analysing how pixels move between existing frames, giving the smoothest slow-motion. Compute-heavy and prone to artefacts on fast or overlapping motion, so it's usually rendered/cached at finishing.

A legacy term, carried over from photochemical film, for shot-level picture effects that aren't a straight cut — dissolves, fades, superimpositions, blow-ups and repositions. Today these are done digitally in the conform/online, but the word still appears on turnover and effects notes.

Full-resolution footage transcoded from a hard-to-decode camera codec into an edit-friendly intermediate (e.g. ProRes or DNxHR) to improve playback and scrubbing without dropping resolution. Distinct from proxies, which trade away resolution; optimised media keeps it.

An open-source timeline interchange format and API created at Pixar and now hosted by the Academy Software Foundation. OTIO stores cut order, timing, tracks, transitions and media references (not the media itself) and uses adapters to read/write EDL, AAF, FCPXML and more, acting as a neutral hub between editorial and finishing/VFX pipelines.

The transform from scene-linear ACES to a display-encoded image for a specific device and standard (e.g. Rec.709 100-nit, Rec.2100 PQ 1000-nit, P3-D65). In ACES 1.x this was the fixed RRT (the filmic 'rendering') followed by a device-specific ODT; ACES 2.0 unifies them under the single term 'Output Transform'.
A complete, fully self-contained IMF or DCP — every track file needed to play the program is inside the package. It is the base on which supplemental Version Files (VF) are built.

DCP package types: an OV (Original Version) is the complete self-contained package; a VF (Version File) is a supplemental carrying only the differences (e.g. alternate language/subtitles) and referencing the OV's assets. Required VFs are built alongside the OV.

The DCI-P3 primaries paired with a D65 (6500K) white point instead of the cinema ~6300K white. This is the working/display gamut for most HDR mastering and consumer wide-gamut screens, because HDR signals carry a D65 white even when the colours are limited to the P3 volume inside a Rec.2020 container.

Cleanup work removing unwanted elements from a plate — rigs, wires, markers, tracking dots, crew, reflections or modern objects in a period scene — using clone, patch and clean-plate techniques. Usually done before comp; 'prep' bundles paint, roto and undistort.

Phase Alternating Line: the 25fps/50Hz broadcast heritage of the UK, Europe, NZ and Australia. NZ drama for TVNZ/Sky is traditionally mastered at 25 fps, though streaming originals increasingly use 23.976 for global release.

Post, Digital and Visual Effects — the NZSPR stream covering post and VFX work. Qualifying PDV includes picture finishing, all VFX, audio post (including Atmos), mastering and deliverables (IMF, DCP, subtitling, QC, forensic watermarking), and project-attributable NZ-invoiced cloud and software spend.

A discretionary extra 5% on top of the 20% base international rebate, assessed by a panel against four roughly equal criteria: industry development, economic benefit, cultural benefit (te ao Māori stories, NZ creative leadership) and tourism/trade. It needs evidence against all four, not one strong story.

The brightest light a display or grade is built to reach. Common HDR mastering peaks are 1,000, 2,000 or 4,000 nits; PQ allows up to 10,000. It is distinct from reference white, which is the much lower nominal diffuse-white level.

An authorised simulated cyber-attack by security specialists to find exploitable weaknesses in a facility's systems before real attackers do. Often required as evidence for TPN/MPA, ISO 27001 or SOC 2 assessments.
+48V DC sent down a balanced XLR cable to power condenser microphones, supplied by the mixer/recorder or preamp. Required by most boom and studio condenser mics; dynamic mics do not use it.

Reconstructing 3D geometry and texture from many overlapping photographs of a subject or location, used to build digital doubles, set models and environments. A primary on-set data-acquisition method alongside LIDAR scanning.

Netflix's open-source library for validating IMF (Interoperable Master Format) and MXF packages, checking that the AssetMap, PackingList, Composition Playlist (CPL) and essence conform to the SMPTE IMF standard. It is widely embedded in vendor tools as a reference IMF validator.

The point at which the edit is creatively and structurally final, releasing the project to sound, VFX and grade to work against a fixed reference. In practice locks still move, which is why downstream changes are tracked via change lists.

The XML manifest of an IMF or DCP listing every file in the package with its size, type and hash (message digest). It is the integrity check used to confirm a package transferred completely and was not corrupted or tampered with.

A source camera shot delivered to VFX as the basis for compositing — typically supplied with handles as DPX or EXR at full resolution in scene-referred colour. Variants include the clean plate (background with elements removed) and reference plates (lighting/HDRI/grey-and-chrome balls).

Extracting the camera-master frames for each VFX shot (with handles) and delivering them to the vendor — typically 16-bit EXR in ACES2065-1 or ACEScg per vendor spec, plus an H.264 offline reference and burn-in metadata.

Microsoft's DRM (Digital Rights Management) system, common on Windows, Edge and smart TVs. It has three security levels: SL150 (software, testing only), SL2000 (software, standard HD), and SL3000 (hardware-backed via a TEE, equivalent to Widevine L1 and required by studios for premium HD/4K).

A set of 3D points produced by a camera solve or LiDAR scan that represents the real-world locations of tracked features. Used by matchmove and layout artists to place and scale CG within the photographed space.

A single Broadcast Wave (BWF) file containing all recorded channels interleaved, as opposed to mono WAVs (one file per channel). The reference production records poly at 48 kHz/24-bit so every ISO mic and the mix sit in one frame-synced file.
Reports to the Post Production Supervisor and handles day-to-day logistics, scheduling, and tracking of post tasks and deliverables. Provides the administrative backbone to the supervisor.

Runs the entire post phase after wrap: builds and tracks the post schedule and budget, coordinates editorial, VFX, sound, music, grade and finishing, owns the deliverables list, and manages QC redeliveries with the broadcaster or platform. The operational nerve centre between production and every downstream vendor.

The producorial counterpart to the post-production supervisor on larger productions, owning creative scheduling, vendor contracting and high-level budget sign-off while the supervisor handles day-to-day execution. On smaller jobs the two roles collapse into one person.

Post-visualisation — rough temp VFX (simple CG, comped greenscreen, animatic elements) cut into the edit after the shoot to show intent and timing before final shots exist. The post-shoot successor to previs; often used to make test screenings readable.

A user-drawn shape (circle, rectangle, polygon or freehand) that isolates part of the frame so a colour correction applies only inside (or outside) it. The colourist's basic tool for local, secondary grading such as darkening a sky or lifting a face.

A display-referred HDR transfer function standardised as SMPTE ST 2084, with absolute code values spanning roughly 0 to 10,000 cd/m² (nits) tuned to human contrast perception. It is the EOTF (Electro-Optical Transfer Function) underpinning HDR10 (10-bit) and Dolby Vision (12-bit); a given nit value always maps to the same code, so it is display-referred, not relative.

The mixing stages: the pre-mix splits and rough-balances each stem, then the final mix balances dialogue, music and effects together in the room to the approved sound. The print masters are output from here.
An intermediate submix of one category — a dialogue, FX or music premix — that consolidates many edited tracks into a manageable, pre-balanced bus ready for the final mix. Also called a predub.

Pre-visualisation — rough 3D animatics built before the shoot to plan camera, staging, action and VFX-heavy sequences. Guides on-set decisions and informs what plates and data VFX will need to capture.

The final, fully-balanced mix in its delivery channel format (e.g. 5.1, 7.1, or Atmos), created by summing the final stems — the master from which release prints and encoded deliverables are made. In Atmos workflows the equivalent master is the ADM BWF.

A performing-rights organisation (e.g. APRA AMCOS, ASCAP, BMI, PRS, SOCAN) that collects and distributes public-performance royalties; CISAC is the international confederation linking them. Each cue on the cue sheet names its PRO.

Audio recorded on set during the shoot — boom and radio/lav mics — delivered as isolated tracks (ISOs) plus a mixdown. The raw material for the dialogue edit; its quality determines how much ADR is needed.
Captures location audio on set, typically to a multitrack recorder such as the Sound Devices Scorpio, and generates the sound reports used to check dailies.

The cinema projector's X.509 security certificate (exported from the TMS) that a KDM is bound to. A frequent KDM failure is the cinema sending the TMS or server cert instead of the projector's, or supplying an expired/revoked Christie or Barco cert.

Apple's family of intra-frame (I-frame-only) mezzanine codecs widely used for acquisition and post 'mastering' because every frame decodes independently, making it fast to edit and grade. ProRes 4444 carries an alpha channel and is the common VFX/finishing variant; 422 HQ is the typical offline/online and broadcast delivery codec.

A lightweight, lower-resolution copy of camera media (e.g. ProRes Proxy or H.264) used for fast offline editing, carrying the same timecode and filenames so the conform can relink to the full-resolution originals later.

A proxy is a low-resolution stand-in for fast offline editing; a mezzanine is a high-quality intermediate (e.g. ProRes, DNxHR) used for finishing and interchange between facilities. Both decouple editing performance and transport from the heavy camera originals.

Photosensitive Epilepsy testing that analyses footage for hazardous flashing and harmful spatial patterns against the Harding/Ofcom criteria (aligned with ITU-R BT.1702). Many broadcasters require a Pass before transmission to protect photosensitive viewers.
The right to publicly perform or communicate a musical work via TV, cinema or streaming, administered by performing-rights organisations such as APRA AMCOS and tracked on the cue sheet.

A list of the exact source clips and frame ranges (including handles) that must be 'pulled' from camera originals and rendered out — typically as DPX or EXR — for VFX or for the DI conform. Generated from the locked cut so the data wrangler or lab renders precisely the needed material.

The repeating field/frame pattern (e.g. 3-2 for film, 2-2-2-4 for some conversions) that fits one frame rate into another. In QC, broken or mixed cadence, caused by editing telecined material without respecting fields, shows as judder or combing and is a common fail.
Venera Technologies' automated file-based QC product, available on-prem and in the cloud, shipping with templates for Netflix, DPP, Amazon Prime, iTunes and CableLabs delivery specs.

The structured technical and content check of a master against a delivery spec before it ships, covering video levels, audio loudness/sync, captions, photosensitivity and artefacts. It combines automated file analysis with human 'eyeball' review; a fail means revisions before acceptance.

A specialist quality-control facility (e.g. Deluxe, Visual Data Media Services, Technicolor or an approved NZ house) that runs structured pre-delivery QC. Netflix and Amazon typically mandate a tier-1 QC house from their approved partner list.

The formal technical pass/fail document accompanying a master, listing checks for video levels, loudness and true peak, caption sync, dropped frames, blanking, PSE/flash and metadata against the delivery spec. Often produced by automated QC (Baton, Vidchecker, Aurora) plus a human eyeball pass, and required before a deliverable is accepted.

Qualifying New Zealand Production Expenditure — the portion of spend that qualifies for the NZSPR rebate. Only project-attributable expenditure invoiced from an NZ supplier counts, per the NZFC definition, and it is capped at NZ$65 million per project. Key minimum thresholds (from Jan 2026): NZ$250k for the PDV grant, NZ$4 M for international live-action, and NZ$20 M to be considered for the ISPR uplift.
Video-assist and playback system that picks up CDLs broadcast from LiveGrade and applies IDT + ODT so on-set playback review matches the live graded image.

A keyer in the grade that selects pixels by hue, saturation and luminance (HSL) so a correction targets only those values — e.g. isolating green foliage or skin. Combined with windows and tracking to build clean secondary corrections.

Redundant Array of Independent Disks. A disk array that combines drives for performance and/or redundancy; the DIT/Data Manager's first on-set backup target before footage is cloned to shuttle drives and LTO.

'Raw' means rendering decisions (white balance, ISO, colour science, look) remain adjustable metadata you can change non-destructively in post. 'Baked' means those decisions are encoded into the pixels and cannot be cleanly undone, as with most ProRes/log deliverables off a debayered signal. The distinction drives how much latitude a colourist actually has.

The engineer who balances all sound-editorial elements into the final mix on the dub stage, delivering the printmaster and discrete stems (dialogue, music, effects) to the required loudness spec and channel layout. The last creative hand on the soundtrack.

ITU-R BT.2020, the ultra-wide gamut defined for UHD and used as the container colour space for HDR delivery. Its primaries are monochromatic points on the spectral locus (red 630 nm, green 532 nm, blue 467 nm) covering about 75.8% of CIE 1931 — far wider than any current display can show, so it is a target container rather than a display capability.

The wide-gamut UHD/HDR standards: ITU-R BT.2020 defines the ultra-wide colour primaries, and ITU-R BT.2100 builds on it for HDR with two transfer functions — PQ (Perceptual Quantizer, SMPTE ST 2084, absolute, up to 10,000 nits) and HLG (Hybrid Log-Gamma, relative/scene-referred). AP1 sits very close to the Rec.2020 gamut.

ITU-R BT.2100, the master HDR standard. It uses the Rec.2020 gamut and D65 white, and defines the two permitted HDR transfer functions (PQ and HLG) plus resolution, frame rate, bit depth (10 or 12) and chroma format. Essentially "Rec.2020 with HDR".

The standard-definition colour space defined by ITU-R BT.601. A common NZ QC trap is SD Rec.601 archive or stock placed in a Rec.709 HD show without conversion, producing a visible colour and saturation mismatch.

The ITU-R standard colour space for HD SDR video, defining the primaries, white point (D65) and roughly 100-nit reference white that most broadcast and web SDR deliverables target. It shares its primaries with sRGB, is the narrowest of the common gamuts, and remains the default SDR delivery space. (Its display gamma is specified separately by BT.1886.)

The standard SDR HD deliverable: ITU-R BT.709 defines the colour primaries and a ~100-nit reference, while ITU-R BT.1886 defines the display transfer (gamma ~2.4) used to view it. In ACES this is the target of the Rec.709 Output Transform/ODT.

RED's wavelet-compressed Bayer RAW codec, written as .R3D and selectable by compression ratio (roughly 3:1 up to 22:1) to trade file size against artefacts. Under the IPP2 pipeline it is encoded in REDWideGamutRGB / Log3G10 and debayered in post.
RED's wide camera colour gamut, paired with the Log3G10 curve on V-Raptor and Komodo cameras.

A discrete segment of a programme — historically one physical film reel (~1000–2000 ft / ~10–20 min), now a logical division — used to break a feature into manageable units for conform, grade and delivery. Each reel typically gets its own EDL, master and QuickTime. Note: within an EDL, 'reel' can also mean the source tape/clip ID.

The official, machine-generated OpenColorIO (OCIO) configuration that implements an ACES release (e.g. the ACES 1.3 or ACES 2.0 reference and 'studio' configs). Using the published config guarantees every department's tools share identical, sanctioned ACES transforms rather than home-rolled variants.

An ACES Look Modification Transform (added in ACES 1.3) that pulls extreme, out-of-gamut camera values back toward the working gamut before grading, taming artefacts on saturated lights, lasers and blue/green screens. Applied early in the chain to keep highly saturated captures controllable.

The fixed monitoring calibration tying a digital level to an acoustic SPL so mixes translate between rooms. Theatrical dub stages calibrate to 85 dB SPL (C-weighted) per channel for a -20 dBFS pink-noise signal (SMPTE RP 200); near-field/streaming rooms use a lower SPL. Without it, loudness judgements don't carry between rooms.

The luminance treated as nominal diffuse/graphics white. SDR is ~100 nits; for HDR, ITU-R BT.2408 sets a reference white of 203 nits on PQ, equivalent to 75% signal on HLG, so graphics and skin tones sit at a consistent, comfortable level across HDR and SDR.

Re-pointing existing timeline clips to media files in a new location or format — for example swapping proxies for camera originals — by changing the file path while keeping the edit intact. Relinking changes where clips point; it does not rebuild the timeline the way a conform does.

Pixel-streaming remote-desktop tech for driving an edit/grade machine from elsewhere. Note HP Anyware (formerly Teradici CAS/PCoIP): HP stops selling it from 7 May 2026, though existing software support runs to 31 October 2029, so many facilities are migrating to Parsec for Teams, NICE DCV, or Jump Desktop, all accepted under current TPN controls. Colour accuracy depends on the GPU/monitor path.

A networked cluster of computers that distributes CG/comp rendering across many machines so frames complete in parallel, managed by a queue scheduler (e.g. Deadline, Tractor). Lets a facility render thousands of frames overnight instead of one workstation grinding for weeks.

The pixel count of capture or delivery. Beware two 4K standards: UHD is 3840x2160; DCI 4K is 4096x2160. On a Bayer sensor, capture resolution (e.g. 6K/8K) exceeds resolved detail, so oversampling 6K to 4K is a common quality strategy. Delivery resolution is set by the deliverable spec, not the sensor.
Resolution is the pixel count of the active picture; raster is the pixel container it sits in. A 1998x1080 flat and a 2048x858 scope image are both in the 2K family but yield different aspect ratios.

A CMOS readout method that exposes the sensor line-by-line rather than all at once, causing skew, wobble or partial-frame flash banding with fast motion or strobes. Distinct from a global shutter, which exposes every pixel simultaneously.

A recording of a location's ambient 'silence' with no action or dialogue, captured on set so the dialogue editor can fill gaps and smooth edits seamlessly. Without it, cut points in dialogue leave audible 'holes' in the background.

Rotoscoping — hand-drawing animated spline shapes frame by frame to create mattes for objects that can't be keyed, e.g. isolating an actor shot against a busy background. Labour-intensive and often the first task turned over to a vendor.

Sending a timeline out to another application (grade, VFX, sound) and bringing the result back into the original edit in sync, via EDL/AAF/XML/OTIO. A clean round trip depends on matching frame rates, timecode and naming so clips line up frame-accurately on return.

Reference Processing Unit. The dynamic-metadata layer carried with a Dolby Vision stream that tells each display how to tone-map scene by scene. Often delivered as a sidecar to an HDR10 base inside an IMF package.

The fixed, device-independent 'film-like' rendering at the heart of the ACES output pipeline, mapping scene-linear ACES into a pleasing display-referred image before the device-specific ODT is applied. In ACES 2.0 the RRT and ODT are merged conceptually into a single Output Transform.
Sony's wide camera colour gamut paired with S-Log3. S-Gamut3.Cine is a slightly smaller variant tuned for an easier grade. Mapped into ACES via the Sony IDT.
Sony's log transfer curve used on Venice, paired with the S-Gamut3 or S-Gamut3.Cine camera gamut. Converted into ACES scene-linear via Sony's own IDT at dailies.

Sony's log curve (S-Log3, modelled on Cineon-style film density) paired with a colour space. S-Gamut3.Cine is the production-friendly gamut, slightly wider than DCI-P3 and easy to grade; S-Gamut3 is the wider full-sensor gamut. The default working space for VENICE/FX-series X-OCN material.

How many times per second audio is digitised, in kHz — 48 kHz is the standard for film/TV (96 kHz for hi-res/music), versus 44.1 kHz for CD. Mismatched sample rates between session and deliverable cause pitch/speed errors if not converted.
Storage Area Network. High-speed shared storage at a post house or VFX facility into which transfer drives are copied so multiple workstations can access the same media simultaneously.

A plain-text caption file format that stores raw CEA-608 caption byte pairs against timecodes. Still widely required for broadcast and some VOD delivery, but limited to the 608 feature set compared with IMSC/TTML.
seeAlso: Subtitles vs SDH vs Closed Captions

definition: Image data whose values are proportional to the light in the original scene, with no display rendering applied — the state ACES2065-1 and ACEScg live in. It has effectively unbounded highlights and is the correct domain for compositing and grading maths, before an Output Transform makes it display-ready.
The 2.39:1 widescreen format for theatrical scope and premium drama, often shot anamorphic. Inside a 16:9 raster it is letterboxed top and bottom.

A pre-release review copy of a cut sent to awards voters, festivals, press, executives or sales agents. Because leaks are common, screeners are now almost always streamed through DRM-protected, session-watermarked platforms rather than physical discs, and are covered by an NDA (Non-Disclosure Agreement).

The governance around pre-release review copies: never send unwatermarked links, never share logins (the mark binds to the user, not the device), revoke access after the window, keep an audit log of every screener, and require a click-through NDA for external reviewers. The Post Supervisor usually owns this.

A post-oriented script analysis that extracts everything driving the post schedule: VFX flags (CG, wire removals, set extensions, green/blue-screen plates), stock/archive references, music cues, stunts/SFX safety passes, animal action (welfare records), te reo content (subtitle scope), and nudity/intimacy (modesty checks).

A report from a clearance firm checking the script for legal exposure (names, trademarks, references) plus a log of every cleared item. Required by E&O underwriters along with fact-check memos (for docs) and signed contributor releases.
Tracks on-set continuity, take selection, and slate/scene/take data, producing the script supervisor notes that dailies are checked against before release.

aka: Subtitles for the Deaf and Hard of Hearing
definition: Subtitles for the Deaf and Hard of Hearing — captions that include dialogue plus non-speech sound descriptors and speaker IDs, delivered as IMSC 1.1/TTML. Distinct from foreign-language subtitles, which translate only spoken dialogue.

Conventional video with a limited luminance range, typically Rec.709 primaries with a BT.1886 gamma at roughly 100-nit reference white. It is the legacy baseline that HDR masters must still be trimmed down to for most broadcast and web delivery.

Colour corrections targeting specific regions or colours of the image (via windows, qualifiers or tracking) applied after the overall primary balance. Used for shaping attention, fixing isolated problems and finessing skin and skies.
Sets the final creative grade in the DI (Digital Intermediate) suite and is the on-set DIT's downstream contact for colour intent. The person to consult on any grade or colour question in finishing.

The chip that converts light into an electrical signal — the digital equivalent of film stock. Almost all cinema cameras use CMOS (Complementary Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor) sensors; the sensor's physical dimensions, photosite count and native sensitivity set resolution, dynamic range and noise floor.

Forensic marks generated per viewing session and tied to a specific user/playback, so each stream of the same file carries a distinct, traceable identifier. It is standard on secure pre-release review platforms for screeners and dailies.

Extending a partially-built physical set with CG or matte painting — adding upper storeys, landscapes or crowds beyond what was constructed. A common 'invisible effects' task that blends a practical foreground into a digital background.

A 1D curve applied before a 3D LUT to redistribute (usually log-encode) wide-dynamic-range linear data into a perceptually even range, so the 3D LUT's limited lattice points land where detail matters. Without a shaper, a 3D LUT applied to scene-linear data wastes nodes and bands the highlights.

Autodesk's VFX and production-tracking platform — originally Shotgun, then ShotGrid, now Flow Production Tracking — used to review shot versions and manage work-in-progress. Ftrack is a common alternative.
The agreed creative look for the production, derived from CDL decisions via an LMT and applied so dailies, on-set monitors and editorial all see a consistent graded image rather than flat log.

Exposure time expressed as a fraction of one frame, inherited from the rotating mechanical shutter disc. The 180° standard exposes for half the frame interval, giving natural motion blur: at 24fps that is 1/(2×24) = 1/48s. Narrower angles (e.g. 45°) sharpen motion into a staccato look; wider angles smear it.
Pomfort's camera-data ingest and QC application. Verifies offloads with checksums, links CDL decisions to clips by timecode/reel, and carries ACES metadata through clip exports.

A quick, rough composite assembled early — often by VFX editorial — to test framing, timing and intent before final shot work begins. Stands in for the finished shot in the cut and helps lock the edit.
The identifying card and countdown placed at the head of a broadcast or online master (e.g. TVNZ's slate plus countdown clock) carrying show info so the broadcaster can ingest and air it correctly.

The UK/NZ-style scene/slate/take numbering and clapperboard convention used to identify shots (e.g. 14A-1-1A). Specified on the Workflow Document so dailies clips can be re-ordered and named consistently.

The per-channel maths of an ASC-CDL grade: out = (in x Slope + Offset) ^ Power, applied independently to R, G and B. Slope acts like gain, Offset like lift/printer-light, and Power like gamma; a single Saturation value is then applied across all three channels.

The standard HD colour-bar test pattern defined in SMPTE RP 219 (RP 219-1 for HD/SD, RP 219-2 for 2K/UHD/8K), used to verify colour, levels and signal path. It is the successor to the legacy SMPTE/EBU 75% and 100% bars.

The current DCP standard (the SMPTE ST 429 family) that has superseded the legacy InterOp DCP, which most major chains have retired. Subtitles are wrapped as MXF track files (ST 429-5) referencing SMPTE-TT/DCDM subtitle XML (ST 428-7). A KDM must match the DCP's standard (SMPTE vs InterOp).

A SMPTE profile of W3C TTML (SMPTE ST 2052-1) that adds bitmap images and binary payloads so legacy CEA-608/708 caption data can be tunnelled losslessly. It was adopted by the US FCC as a safe-harbour caption format and is one of the parent formats IMSC was designed to consolidate.

A US AICPA (American Institute of Certified Public Accountants) audit report on a service provider's controls against the Trust Services Criteria (security, availability, processing integrity, confidentiality, privacy). Cloud and SaaS post vendors often hold SOC 2 Type II reports, which support — but do not replace — a TPN (Trusted Partner Network) assessment.

The calculation step in matchmove where software derives the camera's path, rotation and lens properties from tracked features, producing a virtual camera and a 3D point cloud. A 'good solve' has low pixel error and points that stick to the plate.

The creative who builds non-musical sonic elements — designed effects, textures, signature creature/vehicle/sci-fi sounds — that don't exist as straightforward recordings. Distinct from an FX editor, who places and syncs library and foley material.

A 32-channel multitrack location sound recorder/mixer common on NZ drama. In the reference workflow it records polyphonic broadcast WAVs at 48 kHz/24-bit, 25 fps timecode, with a mono mix track and isolated (ISO) tracks per mic.

The location sound recordist's log detailing each take's scene/slate, channel-to-track/character assignments, mic notes, sample rate and any circled (preferred) takes. It tells the editor and mixer what every audio track contains.

Statement of Work — the contract document defining a vendor's deliverables and scope. On rebate-eligible shows it identifies the NZSPR project code and audit obligations; the Post Supervisor owns these templates under the managed-service model.

The Screen Production and Development Association, the industry body representing New Zealand's independent screen producers in advocacy, training and collective bargaining. It negotiates standard terms with the actors' and crew guilds that shape NZ production paperwork.

The document defining the exact technical parameters each deliverable must meet — codec, container, resolution, frame rate, colour space and transfer function, bit depth, audio channel mapping, loudness target and naming convention. Read it before delivery; QC tests against it.

Altering a clip's playback speed — a retime (a constant speed change) or a speed ramp (speed varying over time, e.g. accelerating into slow-mo). Smooth results on stretched footage usually need frame interpolation (optical flow) rather than simple frame duplication, and are typically finalised in the online/grade rather than offline.

Conventional (non-squeezed) lenses that record the scene at the sensor's native geometry, with no desqueeze required. Wide aspect ratios are achieved by cropping the frame rather than optical squeeze; this is the default look against which anamorphic is contrasted.

Unwanted green or blue light bouncing off a chroma screen onto the foreground subject's edges, hair and shiny surfaces. Despill (spill suppression) neutralises it during keying so the subject sits believably over the new background.

A review screening where the director, sound supervisor, composer and editors watch the locked cut and flag every moment needing work — lines for ADR, sound-design beats, music cues, foley emphasis. Produces the spotting notes that drive the whole audio-post schedule.
The standard colour space for web, computer displays and stills (IEC 61966-2-1). It shares Rec.709 primaries but uses a different transfer curve, so it is not interchangeable with Rec.709 video.

The simplest common subtitle format: a plain-text file of numbered cues with start/end timecodes and text, with almost no styling or positioning. Ubiquitous for web and rough delivery, but rarely accepted as a broadcast or theatrical master format.
seeAlso: Subtitles vs SDH vs Closed Captions

definition: Single Sign-On — one authenticated identity (via an identity provider) granting access to many applications without re-logging in, centralising account control and offboarding. Common in studio security frameworks, usually paired with MFA.

Fixed HDR metadata for a whole title: SMPTE ST 2086 mastering-display colour volume (primaries, white point, min/max luminance) plus MaxCLL (Maximum Content Light Level, the brightest single pixel) and MaxFALL (Maximum Frame-Average Light Level). Required alongside HDR10 deliverables so displays can tone-map correctly.

Stems are the separated final mix buses — Dialogue, Music and Effects (DM&E) — kept discrete so a title can be re-versioned, dubbed or re-balanced without remixing. The print master is the combined, channel-mapped final mix delivered for distribution.

Submixes of a single category or subgroup (e.g. dialogue, music, backgrounds) printed in the final monitoring format (5.1, 7.1, or Atmos beds plus objects) that sum exactly to the final mix. Delivered so the mix can be partially reconstructed or repurposed without remixing.

A downmix that sums a 5.1/7.1 surround mix to two channels (Lt/Rt or Lo/Ro) for stereo playback, using defined coefficients so dialogue and balance survive. Required as a deliverable so surround content plays correctly on stereo devices.
EBU STL — a legacy subtitle file format (EBU Tech 3264) still accepted by TVNZ for older pipelines; new commissions use IMSC 1.1/TTML.

A unit of exposure where one stop doubles or halves the light. The f-stop is the geometric aperture ratio (focal length / pupil diameter); the T-stop (transmission stop) is the measured light actually passing through the lens, so cine lenses are marked in T-stops for reliable exposure matching across a set. One stop equals 0.3 of ND density.

term: Subtitles vs SDH vs Closed Captions
aka: Subtitles for the Deaf and Hard of Hearing
definition: Three distinct text deliverables. Subtitles render dialogue only (usually translated). SDH (Subtitles for the Deaf and Hard of Hearing) add speaker IDs and key sound effects while keeping subtitle styling. Closed Captions are the broadcast accessibility track (CEA-608/708 lineage), toggleable by the viewer, carrying full dialogue plus non-speech audio cues.

The long-dominant cinema sensor/film format, roughly 24x18mm (about 28-30mm diagonal), echoing 35mm motion-picture film. It is the coverage target for the vast majority of cine prime and zoom sets and gives the classic cinematic field of view and depth of field.

Head of the sound-editorial team who designs and oversees all sound elements — dialogue edit, ADR, Foley, sound effects and backgrounds — shaping the project's sonic identity before it reaches the mix. Manages the sound editors and reports to the director and re-recording mixer.

A partial IMF/DCP carrying only what differs from an OV — e.g. a new language audio, alternate subtitles, replacement (pickup) shots, or textless elements — that must be played alongside the OV's track files. It is the mechanism that avoids re-delivering an entire program for every version.

On-set measurements, reference photos, LiDAR scans, witness-camera footage and HDRIs captured to support VFX — feeding accurate matchmove, set reconstruction and lighting. Includes set dimensions, camera height/tilt, lens data and chrome/grey ball references.

Synchronisation licence: permission from the song's publisher/songwriter to set a musical composition in timed relation to picture. It covers the underlying song (notes and lyrics), not a specific recording; for a commercial track you also need the master licence.

A signed consent from an on-camera (or recorded) person granting the production the right to use their performance, image, voice and likeness in the work and its marketing. It feeds the chain of title and is required for E&O; for minors a parent/guardian signs.
The Māori name of the New Zealand Film Commission, the government agency that funds and promotes NZ film and certifies productions for the screen production rebate. Same body referred to as NZFC.
The legacy NZ broadcast caption carriage — subtitles on teletext page 801 (or supplied as an MXF sidecar) accepted by TVNZ for some shows. IMSC 1.1 is now standard for new commissions.

A 'temp' is a placeholder shot (rough comp, postvis or stock) used in screenings and test cuts; the 'final' is the approved, finished version delivered for the master. Editorial tracks which shots are still temp so none slip into delivery.

Wireless video transmission system for on-set monitoring. Bolt sends the SDI (Serial Digital Interface) feed; COLR applies CDL + ODT over the network so the director's monitor shows the graded look on a calibrated display.

Master sections supplied without any burnt-in titles, captions or lower-thirds (and the clean backgrounds behind them), appended to the deliverable so distributors can re-create localised text for other territories. A near-universal contractual deliverable.
seeAlso: Subtitles vs SDH vs Closed Captions

definition: The HH:MM:SS:FF frame address (defined by SMPTE ST 12) stamped on every frame to identify and sync media across editorial, sound and finishing. Comes in drop-frame (29.97/59.94 fps, semicolon separators, skips frame numbers to stay clock-accurate) and non-drop variants — mixing them silently breaks conforms.

The green-to-magenta axis of white balance, perpendicular to the warm/cool colour-temperature axis, used to neutralise casts from fluorescent or LED sources. Together colour temperature and tint fully define a white-balance correction.

The cinema's playback-scheduling system (e.g. Doremi/Dolby, GDC) that imports KDMs, schedules shows and exports the projector certificate. KDMs are pushed to it via portals such as Deluxe One or GDC CineCloud.

Re-mapping an image's luminance to fit a different display range, e.g. squeezing a 4,000-nit grade onto a 600-nit TV, or an HDR master down to SDR. Dynamic metadata exists to guide it scene by scene so highlights roll off without clipping or dulling.

The MPA-owned global content-security assessment programme for media and entertainment vendors. Facilities register, complete a questionnaire against the MPA Best Practices, and are assessed by accredited assessors; passing earns a Shield status studios check before sending you their content.

Reconstructing the real camera (and/or object) motion in 3D from the footage so CG elements lock to the plate. 2D tracking follows points in screen space; matchmove ('3D track') solves a virtual camera and point cloud so renders sit convincingly in the scene.

Physical markers (crosses, dots, tennis balls, tape) placed on green screens or set surfaces to give the tracker reliable, high-contrast features to follow. Removed later in paint/prep; marker spacing and survey aid the solve.

The maths that maps light to code values and back. The OETF (Opto-Electronic Transfer Function) encodes scene light to signal at the camera; the EOTF (Electro-Optical Transfer Function) decodes signal to display light at the screen; the OOTF (Opto-Optical Transfer Function) is the overall scene-to-screen rendering. Choosing the right one (gamma, PQ or HLG) is what makes an image look correct.

A grading session that creates a secondary version of an HDR master for a lower target (e.g. a 100-nit SDR or 600-nit HDR trim) so the look holds up on less capable screens. In Dolby Vision these trims are stored as metadata rather than separate renders.

The maximum inter-sample peak of an audio signal, measured in decibels relative to full scale (dBTP) using oversampling so it catches peaks that fall between samples and would otherwise clip after D-to-A conversion or lossy encoding. EBU R128 caps it at -1 dBTP; ATSC A/85 caps it at -2 dBTP. It is stricter than a simple sample peak (dBFS).

Timed Text Markup Language — the W3C XML subtitle/caption format used for modern delivery. IMSC 1.1 is the constrained TTML profile platforms require (Netflix and Amazon both mandate it). The pipeline must be UTF-8/Unicode-safe so te reo Māori macrons render correctly.

The packaged handoff from editorial to a downstream department — VFX, sound or DI — containing the relevant EDL/AAF/XML, reference QuickTimes, pull and count sheets, and notes for the shots or reels being delivered. Each turnover is versioned so later changes can be reconciled against it via change lists.

Editorial's hand-off of everything a VFX facility needs to make a shot — high-res plates (with handles), cut references, count sheets, EDLs/AAFs, notes and reference QuickTimes — packaged to the vendor's spec. Marks the formal start of shot work on those plates.

A 360-degree rotating render of a CG asset under reference lighting used to review modelling, texturing and look-dev from all angles before a model is approved. Standard sign-off deliverable for assets.
Ultra High Definition: the 3840x2160 (16:9) consumer and streaming '4K' raster. Netflix and Amazon 4K streaming deliverables use UHD, not DCI 4K (4096x2160) — do not confuse the two.
The 2.00:1 aspect ratio (Vittorio Storaro's format) now common on Netflix and other streaming drama (e.g. The Crown) as a middle ground between 16:9 and 2.39 scope.

Universal Scene Description — Pixar's open framework and file format for describing, layering and interchanging large 3D scenes across DCC apps, now the backbone of modern pipelines and LED-volume/virtual-production workflows. Lets multiple departments collaborate non-destructively on one scene.

Universally Unique Identifier: the unique ID every essence file and composition carries in IMF and DCP. CPLs reference assets by UUID; a missing or mismatched UUID (e.g. a KDM tied to an old CPL build) breaks playback and fails QC.

Panasonic's log curve (V-Log, 14+ stops) and its V-Gamut colour space, which fully encompasses BT.2020. Native to the VariCam and used (as the lighter V-Log L) across Lumix bodies; 18% grey sits at 10-bit code value 433.

A circular scope that plots colour as hue (angle) and saturation (distance from centre), with target boxes for the six primary/secondary colours. Used to check white balance, skin-tone line and whether colours sit inside legal gamut.

Sony's full-frame cinema cameras. VENICE/VENICE 2 (dual-base ISO 800/3200, internal X-OCN recording on VENICE 2) is the high-end studio body; BURANO is the smaller single-operator full-frame camera with in-body stabilisation and autofocus. Both shoot in S-Log3 / S-Gamut3.Cine and record X-OCN and/or ProRes.
Editorial specialist who compiles the VFX shot list from the locked offline, manages plate references and burn-ins, and tracks shot versions back into the cut.

Manages the schedule, budget, bidding and vendor relationships for all visual-effects work, tracking shot count and turnover from editorial to VFX houses and back. Partners with the VFX supervisor, who owns creative and technical execution.

A plate is the source camera shot handed to VFX; a pull is the act of extracting those exact frames at full quality with correct handles and colour management for the vendor. Accurate pulls with consistent colour are essential so comps drop back into the conform seamlessly.

A burned-in viewing copy of a shot or reel sent with a turnover so VFX/DI can see the intended cut point, framing, temp effects and timecode/frame counts without opening editorial's project. It communicates intent; the actual conform runs off the accompanying EDL/AAF/XML.

The creative and technical lead for visual effects: on set advising on plate photography and capturing reference and data (HDRIs, camera and lens metadata), and in post directing the look and integration of every VFX shot across vendors. Distinct from the VFX producer, who handles schedule and budget.
Telestream's automated QC and correction tool that can both detect and auto-fix certain faults (loudness, video levels) inline within a transcode workflow.

Darkening or hard cut-off toward the frame corners, most often because the lens image circle is smaller than the sensor area being read. Hard mechanical vignetting from undersized coverage is distinct from cosmetic optical falloff and usually forces a crop or a different lens/sensor mode.

An overt on-screen overlay (recipient name, email, 'PROPERTY OF...', timecode) applied to review copies and screeners to deter casual sharing. Cheap and immediate but removable by cropping, so it is usually paired with forensic (invisible) marking on sensitive content.

Isolating content-handling machines on dedicated VLANs (Virtual Local Area Networks) separate from office and internet networks. A core CSBP requirement so production media cannot be reached from general corporate systems.

Non-specific background crowd murmur and chatter recorded or performed to fill scenes with people (restaurants, stadiums, streets) without intelligible lines. A standard backgrounds/loop-group element in the sound mix.

A scope that plots image luminance (and optionally chroma) vertically against horizontal screen position, used to check exposure, black/white levels and broadcast-legal range. The primary tool DITs and colourists use to judge levels objectively rather than by eye on an uncalibrated display.

The chromaticity a display reproduces as neutral white, setting the overall colour temperature of the image. Different standards fix different white points: D65 for TV/HDR, ~6300K (DCI white) for cinema projection.

Any gamut significantly larger than Rec.709, in practice DCI-P3 or Rec.2020. WCG and HDR usually travel together in modern UHD delivery but are independent: one is about colour range, the other about brightness range.

Google's multi-platform DRM, with three robustness levels: L1 (decrypt, decode and render inside a hardware Trusted Execution Environment, required for studio HD/4K), L2 (hardware decrypt, software render) and L3 (software-only, typically capped to SD). Content is usually AES-128 encrypted, and the level a device reports dictates the maximum resolution a studio will license to it.

aka: Women in Film and Television NZ
definition: Women in Film & Television New Zealand — a national membership body supporting and advocating for women across the screen sector through events, training and networking. A key professional organisation in the NZ industry.

A secondary camera (or several) recording the action from another angle to give VFX extra reference for tracking, timing and 3D reconstruction. Not used in the cut — purely a data/reference capture on set.

The single source-of-truth technical reference for a production, created by the Post Supervisor and distributed to all departments before the shoot. Captures every agreed parameter — capture format, frame rate, colour pipeline, contacts, and deliverable spec.
Post-team specialist who defines and troubleshoots the technical pipeline — colour-managed transforms, transcode specs, conform path — often bridging the DIT, dailies lab, and finishing facility.

The resolution and active image area the production carries through post for the master, chosen relative to the captured sensor area and the framing/extraction defined by the FDL — balancing reframe headroom, VFX cost and delivery requirements. Set it early, because it constrains the whole finishing pipeline.

A core ACES distinction: the interchange space (ACES2065-1, AP0, linear) is what you archive and pass between facilities, while working spaces (ACEScg for VFX, ACEScct/ACEScc for grading, all AP1) are what you actually operate in. You convert to a working space to do the job, then back to interchange to deliver.

aka: eXtended tonal range Original Camera Negative
definition: Sony's 16-bit compressed RAW (eXtended tonal range Original Camera Negative) for VENICE and BURANO, wrapped in MXF at three quality tiers: XT (highest, VFX), ST (standard) and LT (smallest). It delivers near-RAW latitude at far smaller sizes and defaults to an S-Gamut3.Cine / S-Log3 working space.
Sony's MPEG-2 I-frame-friendly broadcast codec at 50 Mbps, 4:2:2 8-bit, wrapped in MXF OP1a. The standard on-air delivery codec for TVNZ and other traditional NZ broadcasters.

The device-independent CIE XYZ-based colour encoding (gamma 2.6) mandated for DCDM/DCP picture, so a film looks consistent across compliant projectors regardless of their native primaries. Grades are typically done in DCI-P3 and then converted to X'Y'Z' for the DCP.
The luma-plus-two-colour-difference encoding used for video signals and broadcast delivery (e.g. YCbCr 4:2:2 10-bit for HD). Editorial Avid projects and HD broadcast masters work in YCbCr 709.

A security model that assumes the network is already breached and grants no implicit trust based on location ('inside the firewall'). Every access request is authenticated, authorised and continuously verified. It is the foundation of MovieLabs CSAP and increasingly expected by studios for cloud post pipelines.
